validation_summary.note: Each of the 4 source JSON files covers a distinct Act/Rules/Direction with no overlapping (act, section_number, subsection_number) entries; therefore no cross-file merging of duplicate sections was required. All entries are preserved 1:1 from their single source file.

sections.title: Short Title
sections.clause_text: This Act may be called the Information Technology Act, 2000.
sections.plain_english_summary: This law is officially named the Information Technology Act, 2000.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 1(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. This Act is officially titled the Information Technology Act, 2000. It provides the legal framework governing electronic commerce, digital signatures, electronic records, and cyber offences in India.

sections.title: Extent and Application
sections.clause_text: It shall extend to the whole of India and, save as otherwise provided in this Act, it applies also to any offence or contravention thereunder committed outside India by any person.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Act applies to the whole of India and also covers offences or contraventions committed outside India by any person.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 1(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Act extends to the whole of India and has extra-territorial application, covering offences or contraventions committed outside India by any person, subject to provisions otherwise specified in the Act.

sections.title: Exclusion of Specified Documents and Transactions
sections.clause_text: Nothing in this Act shall apply to documents or transactions specified in the First Schedule: Provided that the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, amend the First Schedule by way of addition or deletion of entries thereto.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Act does not apply to documents or transactions listed in the First Schedule. The Central Government may amend this list by notification.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 1(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Act does not apply to documents or transactions specified in the First Schedule, which includes instruments like negotiable instruments, powers of attorney, trusts, wills, and contracts for immovable property. The Central Government may amend the First Schedule by notification.

sections.title: Definition - Access
sections.clause_text: "access" with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions means gaining entry into, instructing or communicating with the logical, arithmetical, or memory function resources of a computer, computer system or computer network.
sections.plain_english_summary: Access means gaining entry into, instructing, or communicating with the logical, arithmetic, or memory functions of a computer, computer system, or computer network.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(a) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines access as gaining entry into, instructing or communicating with the logical, arithmetical, or memory function resources of a computer, computer system or computer network. This definition is relevant to offences involving unauthorised access to computer systems.

sections.title: Definition - Addressee
sections.clause_text: "addressee" means a person who is intended by the originator to receive the electronic record but does not include any intermediary.
sections.plain_english_summary: An addressee is the person the originator intends to receive the electronic record. Intermediaries are excluded from this definition.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(b) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines addressee as the intended recipient of an electronic record from the originator, explicitly excluding intermediaries from this category.

sections.title: Definition - Adjudicating Officer
sections.clause_text: "adjudicating officer" means an adjudicating officer appointed under sub-section (1) of section 46.
sections.plain_english_summary: An adjudicating officer is an officer appointed under section 46(1) to determine contraventions and penalties under the Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(c) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines adjudicating officer as a person appointed under section 46(1) to hear and adjudicate contraventions of the Act and award penalties or compensation.

sections.title: Definition - Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: "Appellate Tribunal" means the Appellate Tribunal referred to in sub-section (1) of section 48.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Appellate Tribunal refers to the body designated under section 48(1) to hear appeals under this Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(da) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines the Appellate Tribunal as the body referred to in section 48(1), which is the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal, empowered to hear appeals against orders of the Controller or adjudicating officers.

sections.title: Definition - Certifying Authority
sections.clause_text: "Certifying Authority" means a person who has been granted a licence to issue a electronic signature Certificate under section 24.
sections.plain_english_summary: A Certifying Authority is a person licensed under section 24 to issue electronic signature certificates.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(g) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines Certifying Authority as a person who has been granted a licence under section 24 to issue electronic signature certificates. Certifying Authorities are regulated by the Controller of Certifying Authorities.

sections.title: Definition - Communication Device
sections.clause_text: "communication device" means cell phones, personal digital assistance or combination of both or any other device used to communicate, send or transmit any text, video, audio or image.
sections.plain_english_summary: A communication device includes cell phones, PDAs, or any other device used to communicate, send, or transmit text, video, audio, or images.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(ha) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines communication device as cell phones, personal digital assistants or combination of both or any other device used to communicate, send or transmit any text, video, audio or image. This definition is relevant to offences under sections 66B, 66C, 66D, and 66E.

sections.title: Definition - Computer
sections.clause_text: "computer" means any electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device or system which performs logical, arithmetic, and memory functions by manipulations of electronic, magnetic or optical impulses, and includes all input, output, processing, storage, computer software or communication facilities which are connected or related to the computer in a computer system or computer network.
sections.plain_english_summary: A computer is any electronic, magnetic, or optical data processing device that performs logical, arithmetic, and memory functions, including all connected input, output, storage, and communication facilities.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(i) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines computer broadly as any electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device performing logical, arithmetic and memory functions, including all connected input, output, processing, storage, software and communication facilities in a computer system or network.

sections.title: Definition - Computer Network
sections.clause_text: "computer network" means the inter-connection of one or more computers or computer systems or communication device through— (i) the use of satellite, microwave, terrestrial line, wire, wireless or other communication media; and (ii) terminals or a complex consisting of two or more interconnected computers or communication device whether or not the inter-connection is continuously maintained.
sections.plain_english_summary: A computer network is an interconnection of computers or communication devices through satellite, microwave, wire, wireless, or other media, regardless of whether the connection is continuously maintained.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(j) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines computer network as the interconnection of one or more computers or communication devices through satellite, microwave, terrestrial line, wire, wireless or other communication media, including terminals or complexes of two or more interconnected computers, whether or not continuously connected.

sections.title: Definition - Computer Resource
sections.clause_text: "computer resource" means computer, computer system, computer network, data, computer data base or software.
sections.plain_english_summary: Computer resource means any computer, computer system, computer network, data, computer database, or software.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(k) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines computer resource as any computer, computer system, computer network, data, computer database or software. This broad definition is foundational to many offences and obligations under the Act.

sections.title: Definition - Controller
sections.clause_text: "Controller" means the Controller of Certifying Authorities appointed under sub-section (1) of section 17.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller refers to the Controller of Certifying Authorities appointed under section 17(1) to regulate Certifying Authorities.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(m) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines Controller as the Controller of Certifying Authorities appointed under section 17(1). The Controller supervises Certifying Authorities, certifies public keys, and maintains standards for issuance of electronic signature certificates.

sections.title: Definition - Cyber Cafe
sections.clause_text: "cyber cafe" means any facility from where access to the internet is offered by any person in the ordinary course of business to the members of the public.
sections.plain_english_summary: A cyber cafe is any facility that offers public internet access in the ordinary course of business.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(na) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines cyber cafe as any facility from where access to the internet is offered by any person in the ordinary course of business to the members of the public.

sections.title: Definition - Cyber Security
sections.clause_text: "cyber security" means protecting information, equipment, devices, computer, computer resource, communication device and information stored therein from unauthorised access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification or destruction.
sections.plain_english_summary: Cyber security means protecting information, devices, computers, and communication devices from unauthorised access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(nb) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines cyber security as protecting information, equipment, devices, computers, computer resources, communication devices and information stored therein from unauthorised access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification or destruction. This definition underpins obligations relating to CERT-In and protected systems.

sections.title: Definition - Data
sections.clause_text: "data" means a representation of information, knowledge, facts, concepts or instructions which are being prepared or have been prepared in a formalised manner, and is intended to be processed, is being processed or has been processed in a computer system or computer network, and may be in any form (including computer printouts magnetic or optical storage media, punched cards, punched tapes) or stored internally in the memory of the computer.
sections.plain_english_summary: Data is any formalised representation of information, knowledge, facts, concepts, or instructions intended for processing in a computer system or network, in any physical or internal storage form.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(o) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines data broadly as any formalised representation of information, knowledge, facts, concepts or instructions intended to be, being, or already processed in a computer system or network. It includes computer printouts, magnetic or optical storage media, punched cards, punched tapes, and internal computer memory.

sections.title: Definition - Digital Signature
sections.clause_text: "digital signature" means authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber by means of an electronic method or procedure in accordance with the provisions of section 3.
sections.plain_english_summary: A digital signature is a method of authenticating an electronic record by a subscriber using an electronic procedure as provided under section 3.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(p) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines digital signature as authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber by means of an electronic method or procedure in accordance with section 3, which requires use of an asymmetric crypto system and hash function.

sections.title: Definition - Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: "electronic form" with reference to information, means any information generated, sent, received or stored in media, magnetic, optical, computer memory, micro film, computer generated micro fiche or similar device.
sections.plain_english_summary: Electronic form means information generated, sent, received, or stored in any magnetic, optical, computer memory, microfilm, or similar medium.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(r) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines electronic form as any information generated, sent, received or stored in magnetic, optical, computer memory, microfilm, computer-generated microfiche or similar media. This definition establishes the scope of electronic records and electronic transactions under the Act.

sections.title: Definition - Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: "electronic record" means data, record or data generated, image or sound stored, received or sent in an electronic form or micro film or computer generated micro fiche.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic record is any data, record, image, or sound stored, received, or sent in electronic form or on microfilm or computer-generated microfiche.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(t) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines electronic record as data, records, images or sounds stored, received or sent in electronic form or microfilm or computer-generated microfiche. Electronic records have legal recognition under the Act as equivalent to paper records.

sections.title: Definition - Electronic Signature
sections.clause_text: "electronic signature" means authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber by means of the electronic technique specified in the Second Schedule and includes digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic signature is authentication of an electronic record using techniques specified in the Second Schedule, and includes digital signatures.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(ta) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines electronic signature as authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber using an electronic technique specified in the Second Schedule, and includes digital signatures. Electronic signatures are legally recognised under section 5 as equivalent to handwritten signatures.

sections.title: Definition - Indian Computer Emergency Response Team
sections.clause_text: "Indian Computer Emergency Response Team" means an agency established under sub-section (1) of Section 70B.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) is the agency established under section 70B(1) as the national agency for cyber security incident response.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(ua) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) as the agency established under section 70B(1) to serve as the national agency for cyber security incident response, analysis, and coordination.

sections.title: Definition - Information
sections.clause_text: "information" includes data, message, text, images, sound, voice, codes, computer programmes, software and data bases or micro film or computer generated micro fiche.
sections.plain_english_summary: Information includes data, messages, text, images, sound, voice, codes, computer programs, software, databases, microfilm, or computer-generated microfiche.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(v) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines information broadly to include data, messages, text, images, sound, voice, codes, computer programmes, software, databases, microfilm and computer-generated microfiche. This expansive definition is central to the Act's coverage of digital content and cyber offences.

sections.title: Definition - Intermediary
sections.clause_text: "intermediary", with respect to any particular electronic records, means any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores or transmits that record or provides any service with respect to that record and includes telecom service providers, network service providers, internet service providers, web-hosting service providers, search engines, online payment sites, online-auction sites, online-market places and cyber cafes.
sections.plain_english_summary: An intermediary is any person who receives, stores, or transmits electronic records on behalf of another, including telecom providers, ISPs, web hosts, search engines, online payment and auction sites, online marketplaces, and cyber cafes.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(w) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines intermediary with respect to electronic records as any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores or transmits electronic records or provides any service with respect to such records. This includes telecom service providers, network service providers, internet service providers, web-hosting service providers, search engines, online payment sites, online-auction sites, online marketplaces and cyber cafes. Intermediaries receive conditional safe harbour under section 79.

sections.title: Definition - Originator
sections.clause_text: "originator" means a person who sends, generates, stores or transmits any electronic message or causes any electronic message to be sent, generated, stored or transmitted to any other person but does not include an intermediary.
sections.plain_english_summary: An originator is a person who sends, generates, stores, or transmits an electronic message, excluding intermediaries.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(za) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines originator as a person who sends, generates, stores or transmits any electronic message or causes such message to be sent, generated, stored or transmitted to any other person, but does not include an intermediary.

sections.title: Definition - Secure System
sections.clause_text: "secure system" means computer hardware, software, and procedure that— (a) are reasonably secure from unauthorised access and misuse; (b) provide a reasonable level of reliability and correct operation; (c) are reasonably suited to performing the intended functions; and (d) adhere to generally accepted security procedures.
sections.plain_english_summary: A secure system is computer hardware, software, and procedures that are reasonably secure from unauthorised access, reliable, suited to their intended functions, and follow generally accepted security procedures.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(ze) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines secure system as computer hardware, software and procedures that are reasonably secure from unauthorised access and misuse, provide a reasonable level of reliability and correct operation, are suited to their intended functions, and adhere to generally accepted security procedures.

sections.title: Definition - Subscriber
sections.clause_text: "subscriber" means a person in whose name the electronic signature Certificate is issued.
sections.plain_english_summary: A subscriber is the person in whose name an electronic signature certificate has been issued.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(zg) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines subscriber as a person in whose name an electronic signature certificate is issued. Subscribers have duties under Chapter VIII including maintaining control of private keys and notifying Certifying Authorities of compromise.

sections.title: Authentication of Electronic Records - Digital Signature
sections.clause_text: Subject to the provisions of this section any subscriber may authenticate an electronic record by affixing his digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: A subscriber may authenticate an electronic record by affixing their digital signature, subject to the provisions of this section.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 3(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. A subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by affixing a digital signature. This establishes the legal basis for digital signatures as a method of authentication for electronic records.

sections.title: Authentication - Asymmetric Crypto System and Hash Function
sections.clause_text: The authentication of the electronic record shall be effected by the use of asymmetric crypto system and hash function which envelop and transform the initial electronic record into another electronic record.
sections.plain_english_summary: Authentication of an electronic record is achieved using an asymmetric crypto system and hash function that transforms the original record into a new authenticated electronic record.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 3(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Authentication of electronic records must be effected using an asymmetric crypto system and hash function that envelops and transforms the initial electronic record into another electronic record. The hash function produces a unique result for each input record, making it computationally infeasible to reconstruct the original from the hash or for two records to produce the same hash.

sections.title: Verification of Electronic Record Using Public Key
sections.clause_text: Any person by the use of a public key of the subscriber can verify the electronic record.
sections.plain_english_summary: Anyone can verify an authenticated electronic record using the subscriber's public key.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 3(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person may verify an authenticated electronic record using the subscriber's public key. This enables third-party verification of digitally signed records without requiring access to the private key.

sections.title: Electronic Signature - Authentication Techniques
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in section 3, but subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), a subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by such electronic signature or electronic authentication technique which— (a) is considered reliable; and (b) may be specified in the Second Schedule.
sections.plain_english_summary: In addition to digital signatures under section 3, a subscriber may authenticate electronic records using any reliable electronic signature or authentication technique specified in the Second Schedule.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 3A(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. A subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by an electronic signature or electronic authentication technique that is considered reliable and is specified in the Second Schedule. This provision broadens authentication methods beyond asymmetric cryptography to include other techniques meeting reliability standards.

sections.title: Reliability Criteria for Electronic Signature Techniques
sections.clause_text: For the purposes of this section any electronic signature or electronic authentication technique shall be considered reliable if— (a) the signature creation data or the authentication data are, within the context in which they are used, linked to the signatory or, as the case may be, the authenticator and to no other person; (b) the signature creation data or the authentication data were, at the time of signing, under the control of the signatory or, as the case may be, the authenticator and of no other person; (c) any alteration to the electronic signature made after affixing such signature is detectable; (d) any alteration to the information made after its authentication by electronic signature is detectable; and (e) it fulfils such other conditions which may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic signature technique is reliable if it uniquely links to the signatory, was under their exclusive control at signing, and any post-signing alterations to the signature or the authenticated information are detectable.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 3A(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000 sets out reliability criteria for electronic signature techniques. A technique is reliable if: the signature creation data is linked exclusively to the signatory; it was under the signatory's exclusive control at the time of signing; any post-signing alteration to the signature is detectable; any post-authentication alteration to the information is detectable; and any other prescribed conditions are fulfilled.

sections.title: Legal Recognition of Electronic Records
sections.clause_text: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be in writing or in the typewritten or printed form, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is— (a) rendered or made available in an electronic form; and (b) accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any legal requirement for information to be in writing or printed form is satisfied if the information is in electronic form and accessible for future reference.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 4 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any legal requirement that information or a matter be in writing, typewritten, or printed form is deemed satisfied if that information is rendered or made available in electronic form and is accessible so as to be usable for subsequent reference. This grants electronic records legal equivalence to paper records.

sections.title: Legal Recognition of Electronic Signatures
sections.clause_text: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be authenticated by affixing the signature or any document shall be signed or bear the signature of any person, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied, if such information or matter is authenticated by means of electronic signature affixed in such manner as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any legal requirement for a handwritten signature is satisfied if the information or document is authenticated using an electronic signature in the manner prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 5 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any legal requirement for authentication by affixing a handwritten signature is satisfied if the information or matter is authenticated by means of an electronic signature affixed in the manner prescribed by the Central Government. This gives electronic signatures the same legal standing as handwritten signatures.

sections.title: Use of Electronic Records in Government - Acceptance
sections.clause_text: Where any law provides for— (a) the filing of any form, application or any other document with any office, authority, body or agency owned or controlled by the appropriate Government in a particular manner; (b) the issue or grant of any licence, permit, sanction or approval by whatever name called in a particular manner; (c) the receipt or payment of money in a particular manner, then, notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment, as the case may be, is effected by means of such electronic form as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Legal requirements for filing documents, issuing licences, or making payments with Government in a particular manner are satisfied if done in the electronic form prescribed by the appropriate Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 6(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any legal requirement for filing forms or documents with Government, issuing licences or permits, or making or receiving payments in a particular manner is deemed satisfied if effected in the electronic form prescribed by the appropriate Government. This enables e-governance and paperless interactions with government bodies.

sections.title: Delivery of Services by Service Provider - Authorisation
sections.clause_text: The appropriate Government may, for the purposes of this Chapter and for efficient delivery of services to the public through electronic means authorise, by order, any service provider to set up, maintain and upgrade the computerised facilities and perform such other services as it may specify, by notification in the Official Gazette.
sections.plain_english_summary: The appropriate Government may authorise service providers by order to set up, maintain, and upgrade computerised facilities for public service delivery through electronic means.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 6A(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The appropriate Government may authorise any service provider by order to set up, maintain and upgrade computerised facilities and perform other specified services for efficient delivery of services to the public through electronic means. Service providers include private agencies, companies, partnership firms and sole proprietors granted permission to offer services through electronic means.

sections.title: Service Provider - Service Charges
sections.clause_text: The appropriate Government may also authorise any service provider authorised under sub-section (1) to collect, retain and appropriate such service charges, as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government for the purpose of providing such services, from the person availing such service.
sections.plain_english_summary: The appropriate Government may permit an authorised service provider to collect, retain, and appropriate prescribed service charges from users.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 6A(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The appropriate Government may authorise service providers authorised under section 6A(1) to collect, retain and appropriate service charges as prescribed, from persons availing the electronic services.

sections.title: Retention of Electronic Records
sections.clause_text: Where any law provides that documents, records or information shall be retained for any specific period, then, that requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such documents, records or information are retained in the electronic form, if— (a) the information contained therein remains accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference; (b) the electronic record is retained in the format in which it was originally generated, sent or received or in a format which can be demonstrated to represent accurately the information originally generated, sent or received; (c) the details which will facilitate the identification of the origin, destination, date and time of despatch or receipt of such electronic record are available in the electronic record.
sections.plain_english_summary: A legal retention requirement for documents is satisfied by retaining them in electronic form, provided the records remain accessible, are kept in original or accurately representative format, and include origin, destination, and date/time details.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 7(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. A legal requirement to retain documents, records or information for a specific period is satisfied if they are retained in electronic form, subject to three conditions: the information remains accessible for subsequent reference; the electronic record is in its original format or one accurately representing the original; and details of origin, destination, date and time of despatch or receipt are available within the record.

sections.title: Audit of Documents Maintained in Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: Where in any law for the time being in force, there is a provision for audit of documents, records or information, that provision shall also be applicable for audit of documents, records or information processed and maintained in the electronic form.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any statutory audit requirement for documents or records applies equally to documents, records, or information maintained in electronic form.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 7A of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any provision in law requiring audit of documents, records or information also applies to documents, records or information that are processed and maintained in electronic form. This ensures audit requirements are technology-neutral.

sections.title: Publication in Electronic Gazette
sections.clause_text: Where any law provides that any rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter shall be published in the Official Gazette, then, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter is published in the Official Gazette or Electronic Gazette.
sections.plain_english_summary: A legal requirement to publish in the Official Gazette is satisfied if publication occurs in either the printed Official Gazette or the Electronic Gazette.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 8 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any legal requirement for publication in the Official Gazette is satisfied if publication is made in either the Official Gazette or the Electronic Gazette. The date of publication is the date of whichever gazette form is published first.

sections.title: No Right to Insist on Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: Nothing contained in sections 6, 7 and 8 shall confer a right upon any person to insist that any Ministry or Department of the Central Government or the State Government or any authority or body established by or under any law or controlled or funded by the Central or State Government should accept, issue, create, retain and preserve any document in the form of electronic records or effect any monetary transaction in the electronic form.
sections.plain_english_summary: Sections 6, 7, and 8 do not give any person the right to demand that Government bodies accept, issue, or preserve documents in electronic form or conduct transactions electronically.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 9 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Despite sections 6, 7, and 8 enabling electronic records, no person has the right to insist that any Government Ministry, Department, authority or body funded or controlled by Central or State Government must accept, issue, create, retain or preserve documents in electronic form or conduct monetary transactions electronically.

sections.title: Validity of Contracts Formed Through Electronic Means
sections.clause_text: Where in a contract formation, the communication of proposals, the acceptance of proposals, the revocation of proposals and acceptances, as the case may be, are expressed in electronic form or by means of an electronic records, such contract shall not be deemed to be unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used for that purpose.
sections.plain_english_summary: Contracts formed through electronic means — including proposals, acceptances, and revocations communicated electronically — are not unenforceable solely because they were made electronically.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 10A of the Information Technology Act 2000. A contract is not unenforceable merely because proposals, acceptances, or revocations were communicated in electronic form or through electronic records. This establishes the legal validity of contracts concluded through electronic means, including e-commerce transactions.

sections.title: Attribution of Electronic Records
sections.clause_text: An electronic record shall be attributed to the originator— (a) if it was sent by the originator himself; (b) by a person who had the authority to act on behalf of the originator in respect of that electronic record; or (c) by an information system programmed by or on behalf of the originator to operate automatically.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic record is attributed to the originator if it was sent by the originator, by an authorised representative, or by an automated system programmed on the originator's behalf.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 11 of the Information Technology Act 2000. An electronic record is attributed to the originator if it was sent by the originator personally, by a person authorised to act on the originator's behalf, or by an automated information system programmed by or on behalf of the originator.

sections.title: Acknowledgment of Receipt of Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: Where the originator has not stipulated that the acknowledgment of receipt of electronic record be given in a particular form or by a particular method, an acknowledgment may be given by— (a) any communication by the addressee, automated or otherwise; or (b) any conduct of the addressee, sufficient to indicate to the originator that the electronic record has been received.
sections.plain_english_summary: Where the originator has not specified how acknowledgment must be given, it may be given by any communication or conduct from the addressee that indicates receipt.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 12(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where the originator has not stipulated a specific form or method for acknowledgment of receipt, acknowledgment may be given by any communication (automated or otherwise) from the addressee or by any conduct of the addressee sufficient to indicate to the originator that the electronic record has been received.

sections.title: Binding Effect Conditional on Acknowledgment
sections.clause_text: Where the originator has stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of an acknowledgment of such electronic record by him, then unless acknowledgment has been so received, the electronic record shall be deemed to have been never sent by the originator.
sections.plain_english_summary: If the originator conditions the binding effect of an electronic record on acknowledgment, the record is deemed never sent until such acknowledgment is received.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 12(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where the originator has stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only upon receipt of an acknowledgment, the record is deemed never to have been sent unless and until that acknowledgment is received by the originator.

sections.title: Time of Despatch of Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, the despatch of an electronic record occurs when it enters a computer resource outside the control of the originator.
sections.plain_english_summary: Unless agreed otherwise, an electronic record is deemed dispatched when it enters a computer resource outside the originator's control.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 13(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Unless otherwise agreed between the originator and addressee, the despatch of an electronic record occurs at the moment it enters a computer resource outside the control of the originator.

sections.title: Time of Receipt of Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: Save as otherwise agreed between the originator and the addressee, the time of receipt of an electronic record shall be determined as follows, namely:— (a) if the addressee has designated a computer resource for the purpose of receiving electronic records,— (i) receipt occurs at the time when the electronic record enters the designated computer resource; or (ii) if the electronic record is sent to a computer resource of the addressee that is not the designated computer resource, receipt occurs at the time when the electronic record is retrieved by the addressee; (b) if the addressee has not designated a computer resource along with specified timings, if any, receipt occurs when the electronic record enters the computer resource of the addressee.
sections.plain_english_summary: Unless agreed otherwise, receipt occurs when the record enters the addressee's designated computer resource, or when retrieved if sent to an undesignated resource. If no resource is designated, receipt occurs upon entry into the addressee's computer resource.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 13(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The time of receipt of an electronic record is determined by whether the addressee has designated a computer resource for receiving records. If a resource is designated, receipt occurs on entry into that resource. If sent to a non-designated resource, receipt occurs on retrieval by the addressee. If no resource is designated, receipt occurs on entry into the addressee's computer resource.

sections.title: Secure Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: Where any security procedure has been applied to an electronic record at a specific point of time, then such record shall be deemed to be a secure electronic record from such point of time to the time of verification.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic record to which a security procedure has been applied is deemed a secure electronic record from the time of application until the time of verification.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 14 of the Information Technology Act 2000. An electronic record to which a security procedure has been applied at a specific point in time is deemed to be a secure electronic record from that point in time until the time of verification. Security procedures are prescribed by the Central Government under section 16.

sections.title: Secure Electronic Signature
sections.clause_text: An electronic signature shall be deemed to be a secure electronic signature if— (i) the signature creation data, at the time of affixing signature, was under the exclusive control of signatory and no other person; and (ii) the signature creation data was stored and affixed in such exclusive manner as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: An electronic signature is a secure electronic signature if the signature creation data was under the exclusive control of the signatory at the time of signing and was stored and affixed in the prescribed exclusive manner.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 15 of the Information Technology Act 2000. An electronic signature is deemed secure if at the time of affixing, the signature creation data was under the exclusive control of the signatory and no other person, and the signature creation data was stored and affixed in the exclusive manner prescribed by the Central Government. For digital signatures, the signature creation data refers to the subscriber's private key.

sections.title: Security Procedures and Practices
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of sections 14 and 15, prescribe the security procedures and practices: Provided that in prescribing such security procedures and practices, the Central Government shall have regard to the commercial circumstances, nature of transactions and such other related factors as it may consider appropriate.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may prescribe security procedures and practices for secure electronic records and signatures under sections 14 and 15, considering commercial circumstances and the nature of transactions.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 16 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government is empowered to prescribe security procedures and practices for the purposes of sections 14 and 15, relating to secure electronic records and secure electronic signatures. In prescribing such procedures, the Central Government shall consider commercial circumstances, the nature of transactions, and other relevant factors.

sections.title: Appointment of Controller of Certifying Authorities
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities for the purposes of this Act and may also by the same or subsequent notification appoint such number of Deputy Controllers, Assistant Controllers, other officers and employees as it deems fit.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities and such Deputy Controllers, Assistant Controllers, and other officers as it sees fit, by notification.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 17(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities and any number of Deputy Controllers, Assistant Controllers, other officers and employees as deemed necessary, by notification in the Official Gazette. The Controller regulates and supervises Certifying Authorities under the Act.

sections.title: Functions of Controller
sections.clause_text: The Controller may perform all or any of the following functions, namely:— (a) exercising supervision over the activities of the Certifying Authorities; (b) certifying public keys of the Certifying Authorities; (c) laying down the standards to be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; (d) specifying the qualifications and experience which employees of the Certifying Authority should possess; (e) specifying the conditions subject to which the Certifying Authorities shall conduct their business; (f) specifying the contents of written, printed or visual materials and advertisements that may be distributed or used in respect of a electronic signature Certificate and the public key; (g) specifying the form and content of a electronic signature Certificate and the key; (h) specifying the form and manner in which accounts shall be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; (i) specifying the terms and conditions subject to which auditors may be appointed; (j) facilitating the establishment of any electronic system by a Certifying Authority; (k) specifying the manner in which the Certifying Authorities shall conduct their dealings with the subscribers; (l) resolving any conflict of interests between the Certifying Authorities and the subscribers; (m) laying down the duties of the Certifying Authorities; (n) maintaining a data base containing the disclosure record of every Certifying Authority.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller supervises Certifying Authorities, sets standards, certifies public keys, specifies conditions for conducting business, resolves disputes, and maintains a public disclosure database of Certifying Authorities.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 18 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller of Certifying Authorities may supervise Certifying Authorities, certify their public keys, set standards and qualifications, specify business conditions and dealing procedures, resolve conflicts of interest with subscribers, maintain a publicly accessible database of disclosure records for all Certifying Authorities, and perform other regulatory functions.

sections.title: Recognition of Foreign Certifying Authorities
sections.clause_text: Subject to such conditions and restrictions as may be specified by regulations, the Controller may with the previous approval of the Central Government, and by notification in the Official Gazette, recognise any foreign Certifying Authority as a Certifying Authority for the purposes of this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may recognise a foreign Certifying Authority as valid under this Act, with prior Central Government approval and subject to prescribed conditions.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 19 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may recognise any foreign Certifying Authority as a Certifying Authority for purposes of the Act, with the prior approval of the Central Government, by notification in the Official Gazette, and subject to conditions and restrictions specified by regulations. Electronic signature certificates issued by such recognised foreign authorities are valid under the Act.

sections.title: Licence to Issue Electronic Signature Certificates
sections.clause_text: Subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), any person may make an application, to the Controller, for a licence to issue electronic signature Certificates. No licence shall be issued under sub-section (1), unless the applicant fulfils such requirements with respect to qualification, expertise, manpower, financial resources and other infrastructure facilities, which are necessary to issue electronic signature Certificates as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person may apply to the Controller for a licence to issue electronic signature certificates, provided they meet prescribed requirements for qualification, expertise, manpower, financial resources, and infrastructure.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 21 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person may apply to the Controller for a licence to issue electronic signature certificates. A licence will only be granted if the applicant satisfies prescribed requirements relating to qualification, expertise, manpower, financial resources and other infrastructure facilities necessary for issuing electronic signature certificates.

sections.title: Suspension and Revocation of Certifying Authority Licence
sections.clause_text: The Controller may, if he is satisfied after making such inquiry, as he may think fit, that a Certifying Authority has— (a) made a statement in, or in relation to, the application for the issue or renewal of the licence, which is incorrect or false in material particulars; (b) failed to comply with the terms and conditions subject to which the licence was granted; (c) failed to maintain the procedures and standards specified in section 30; (d) contravened any provisions of this Act, rule, regulation or order made thereunder, revoke the licence.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may revoke a Certifying Authority's licence if it made false statements in its application, failed to comply with licence conditions, failed to maintain required standards, or violated the Act. The Controller may also suspend a licence pending inquiry.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 25 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may revoke a Certifying Authority licence after inquiry if the authority made false statements in its licence application, failed to comply with licence conditions, failed to maintain procedures and standards under section 30, or contravened any provision of the Act. A licence may also be suspended for up to ten days pending completion of an inquiry, with opportunity to show cause.

sections.title: Certifying Authority - Mandatory Procedures
sections.clause_text: Every Certifying Authority shall,— (a) make use of hardware, software and procedures that are secure from intrusion and misuse; (b) provide a reasonable level of reliability in its services which are reasonably suited to the performance of intended functions; (c) adhere to security procedures to ensure that the secrecy and privacy of the electronic signatures are assured; (ca) be the repository of all electronic signature Certificates issued under this Act; (cb) publish information regarding its practices, electronic signature Certificates and current status of such certificates; and (d) observe such other standards as may be specified by regulations.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every Certifying Authority must use secure hardware and software, provide reliable services, maintain security of electronic signatures, act as a certificate repository, publish certificate status information, and comply with regulatory standards.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 30 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every Certifying Authority has a mandatory obligation to use hardware, software and procedures that are secure from intrusion and misuse, provide reliable services, ensure secrecy and privacy of electronic signatures, act as a repository for all electronic signature certificates it issues, publish information about its practices and certificate status, and observe standards specified by regulations.

sections.title: Certifying Authority - Ensure Compliance by Employees
sections.clause_text: Every Certifying Authority shall ensure that every person employed or otherwise engaged by it complies, in the course of his employment or engagement, with the provisions of this Act, rules, regulations and orders made thereunder.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every Certifying Authority must ensure that all its employees and engaged persons comply with the Act and rules made thereunder during the course of their work.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 31 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every Certifying Authority is obligated to ensure that every person employed or engaged by it complies with the provisions of the Act and all rules, regulations and orders made under it, in the course of their employment or engagement.

sections.title: Certifying Authority - Disclosure Obligations
sections.clause_text: Every Certifying Authority shall disclose in the manner specified by regulations— (a) its electronic signature Certificate; (b) any certification practice statement relevant thereto; (c) notice of the revocation or suspension of its Certifying Authority certificate, if any; and (d) any other fact that materially and adversely affects either the reliability of a electronic signature Certificate, which that Authority has issued, or the Authority's ability to perform its services.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every Certifying Authority must disclose its electronic signature certificate, certification practice statement, any revocation or suspension of its certificate, and any material facts adversely affecting certificate reliability or service performance.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 34 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every Certifying Authority must disclose its own electronic signature certificate, relevant certification practice statements, notice of revocation or suspension of its certificate, and any fact that materially and adversely affects the reliability of certificates it has issued or its ability to perform services.

sections.title: Application for Electronic Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: Any person may make an application to the Certifying Authority for the issue of a electronic signature Certificate in such form as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person may apply to a Certifying Authority for an electronic signature certificate using the form prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 35(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person may apply to a Certifying Authority for the issue of an electronic signature certificate in the form prescribed by the Central Government.

sections.title: Suspension of Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: Subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), the Certifying Authority which has issued a Digital Signature Certificate may suspend such Digital Signature Certificate,— (a) on receipt of a request to that effect from— (i) the subscriber listed in the Digital Signature Certificate; or (ii) any person duly authorised to act on behalf of that subscriber; (b) if it is of opinion that the Digital Signature Certificate should be suspended in public interest. A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be suspended for a period exceeding fifteen days unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard in the matter.
sections.plain_english_summary: A Certifying Authority may suspend a Digital Signature Certificate on the subscriber's request or in the public interest, but not for more than fifteen days unless the subscriber has been given a hearing.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 37 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A Certifying Authority may suspend a Digital Signature Certificate on request from the subscriber or their authorised representative, or in the public interest. A suspension cannot exceed fifteen days unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity to be heard.

sections.title: Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it— (a) where the subscriber or any other person authorised by him makes a request to that effect; or (b) upon the death of the subscriber; or (c) upon the dissolution of the firm or winding up of the company where the subscriber is a firm or a company. A Certifying Authority may also revoke a certificate if a material fact is false, a requirement for issuance was not satisfied, the Certifying Authority's security was compromised, or the subscriber is declared insolvent or has ceased to exist. A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be revoked unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard.
sections.plain_english_summary: A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate on subscriber request, death, dissolution, falsity of material facts, unmet issuance requirements, security compromise, or insolvency, subject to giving the subscriber an opportunity to be heard.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 38 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate on subscriber request, on the death or dissolution of the subscriber, or if a material fact is false or concealed, an issuance requirement was not satisfied, the Certifying Authority's security system was compromised, or the subscriber is insolvent or has ceased to exist. Revocation requires giving the subscriber an opportunity to be heard.

sections.title: Subscriber - Duty to Generate Key Pair
sections.clause_text: Where any Digital Signature Certificate the public key of which corresponds to the private key of that subscriber which is to be listed in the Digital Signature Certificate has been accepted by a subscriber, the subscriber shall generate that key pair by applying the security procedure.
sections.plain_english_summary: A subscriber who accepts a Digital Signature Certificate must generate the corresponding key pair using the prescribed security procedure.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 40 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where a subscriber accepts a Digital Signature Certificate, they are obligated to generate the corresponding key pair by applying the prescribed security procedure. The key pair consists of the private key used to create the digital signature and the public key listed in the certificate.

sections.title: Subscriber - Control of Private Key
sections.clause_text: Every subscriber shall exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in his Digital Signature Certificate and take all steps to prevent its disclosure.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every subscriber must take reasonable care to maintain control of their private key and take all steps to prevent its disclosure to unauthorised persons.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 42(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every subscriber has a duty to exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in their Digital Signature Certificate and to take all steps to prevent its disclosure. The subscriber remains liable until they have notified the Certifying Authority of a compromise.

sections.title: Subscriber - Duty to Report Private Key Compromise
sections.clause_text: If the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate has been compromised, then, the subscriber shall communicate the same without any delay to the Certifying Authority in such manner as may be specified by the regulations.
sections.plain_english_summary: If a subscriber's private key is compromised, they must immediately notify the Certifying Authority in the manner specified by regulations. The subscriber remains liable until such notification is made.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 42(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. If a subscriber's private key is compromised, the subscriber must immediately communicate this to the Certifying Authority without any delay, in the manner specified by regulations. The subscriber remains liable for any use of the compromised key until the Certifying Authority is notified.

sections.title: Penalty and Compensation for Damage to Computer Systems
sections.clause_text: If any person without permission of the owner or any other person who is in charge of a computer, computer system or computer network,— (a) accesses or secures access to such computer, computer system or computer network or computer resource; (b) downloads, copies or extracts any data, computer data base or information; (c) introduces or causes to be introduced any computer contaminant or computer virus; (d) damages or causes to be damaged any computer, computer system or computer network, data, computer data base or any other programmes; (e) disrupts or causes disruption of any computer, computer system or computer network; (f) denies or causes the denial of access to any person authorised to access; (g) provides any assistance to any person to facilitate access in contravention of the provisions of this Act; (h) charges services to the account of another person by tampering; (i) destroys, deletes or alters any information residing in a computer resource or diminishes its value; (j) steal, conceal, destroys or alters any computer source code, he shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person who, without permission, accesses, damages, disrupts, introduces viruses into, or tampers with a computer system or its data is liable to pay compensation to the affected person.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 43 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person who without the permission of the owner or person in charge accesses, downloads from, introduces contaminants into, damages, disrupts, denies access to, assists unauthorised access to, manipulates billing, destroys or alters information in, or tampers with computer source code on a computer, computer system or computer network is liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person affected.

sections.title: Compensation for Failure to Protect Data
sections.clause_text: Where a body corporate, possessing, dealing or handling any sensitive personal data or information in a computer resource which it owns, controls or operates, is negligent in implementing and maintaining reasonable security practices and procedures and thereby causes wrongful loss or wrongful gain to any person, such body corporate shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate that negligently fails to implement reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data and thereby causes wrongful loss or gain to any person is liable to pay compensation to that person.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 43A of the Information Technology Act 2000. A body corporate that possesses, deals with or handles sensitive personal data or information in a computer resource and is negligent in implementing and maintaining reasonable security practices and procedures is liable to pay compensation if that negligence causes wrongful loss or wrongful gain to any person. Reasonable security practices means practices designed to protect information from unauthorised access, damage, use, modification, disclosure or impairment as specified by agreement, law, or prescribed by the Central Government.

sections.title: Penalty for Failure to Furnish Information
sections.clause_text: If any person who is required under this Act or any rules or regulations made thereunder to— (a) furnish any document, return or report to the Controller or the Certifying Authority fails to furnish the same, he shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding one lakh and fifty thousand rupees for each such failure; (b) file any return or furnish any information, books or other documents within the time specified therefor in the regulations fails to file return or furnish the same within the time specified, he shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding five thousand rupees for every day during which such failure continues; (c) maintain books of account or records, fails to maintain the same, he shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding ten thousand rupees for every day during which the failure continues.
sections.plain_english_summary: Failure to furnish required documents to the Controller or Certifying Authority attracts a penalty up to Rs 1.5 lakh. Failure to file returns within the specified time attracts up to Rs 5,000 per day. Failure to maintain books of account attracts up to Rs 10,000 per day.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 44 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Penalties for failure to comply with information furnishing obligations: failure to furnish documents or reports to the Controller or Certifying Authority attracts a penalty of up to Rs 1,50,000 per failure; failure to file returns or furnish information within the prescribed time attracts up to Rs 5,000 per day of continuing failure; failure to maintain required books of account or records attracts up to Rs 10,000 per day of continuing failure.

sections.title: Residuary Penalty
sections.clause_text: Whoever contravenes any rules or regulations made under this Act, for the contravention of which no penalty has been separately provided, shall be liable to pay a compensation not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees to the person affected by such contravention or a penalty not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any contravention of rules or regulations under the Act for which no specific penalty is provided attracts a residual compensation or penalty of up to Rs 25,000.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 45 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A residuary penalty provision: whoever contravenes any rules or regulations made under the Act for which no separate penalty is prescribed shall be liable to pay compensation not exceeding Rs 25,000 to the affected person or a penalty not exceeding Rs 25,000.

sections.title: Power to Adjudicate - Appointment of Adjudicating Officer
sections.clause_text: For the purpose of adjudging under this Chapter whether any person has committed a contravention of any of the provisions of this Act or of any rule, regulation, direction or order made thereunder which renders him liable to pay penalty or compensation, the Central Government shall, subject to the provisions of sub-section (3), appoint any officer not below the rank of a Director to the Government of India or an equivalent officer of a State Government to be an adjudicating officer for holding an inquiry in the manner prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government appoints an officer of at least Director rank as adjudicating officer to conduct inquiries into contraventions of the Act and determine liability for penalties or compensation.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 46(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government appoints an officer not below the rank of a Director to the Government of India, or an equivalent State Government officer, as adjudicating officer to adjudicate contraventions of the Act and determine liability for penalties or compensation. The adjudicating officer's jurisdiction is limited to claims not exceeding Rs 5 crore, with higher claims going to the competent court.

sections.title: Adjudicating Officer - Pecuniary Jurisdiction
sections.clause_text: The adjudicating officer appointed under sub-section (1) shall exercise jurisdiction to adjudicate matters in which the claim for injury or damage does not exceed rupees five crore: Provided that the jurisdiction in respect of the claim for injury or damage exceeding rupees five crores shall vest with the competent court.
sections.plain_english_summary: The adjudicating officer has jurisdiction over claims up to Rs 5 crore. Claims exceeding Rs 5 crore are within the jurisdiction of the competent court.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 46(1A) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The adjudicating officer's jurisdiction is limited to claims for injury or damage not exceeding Rs 5 crore. Claims exceeding Rs 5 crore vest with the competent court. This creates a tiered adjudication framework.

sections.title: Factors for Adjudicating Compensation
sections.clause_text: While adjudging the quantum of compensation under this Chapter, the adjudicating officer shall have due regard to the following factors, namely:— (a) the amount of gain of unfair advantage, wherever quantifiable, made as a result of the default; (b) the amount of loss caused to any person as a result of the default; (c) the repetitive nature of the default.
sections.plain_english_summary: In determining the quantum of compensation, the adjudicating officer must consider the unfair advantage gained, the loss caused to any person, and whether the default is repetitive.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 47 of the Information Technology Act 2000. When adjudging the quantum of compensation, the adjudicating officer must consider: the amount of unfair advantage gained by the defaulter; the amount of loss caused to any person; and the repetitive nature of the default. These factors guide proportionate compensation awards.

sections.title: Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: The Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal established under section 14 of the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India Act, 1997, shall, on and from the commencement of Part XIV of Chapter VI of the Finance Act, 2017, be the Appellate Tribunal for the purposes of this Act and the said Appellate Tribunal shall exercise the jurisdiction, powers and authority conferred on it by or under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT) serves as the Appellate Tribunal for the purposes of the IT Act, exercising all jurisdiction and powers conferred by the Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 48(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT) established under the TRAI Act 1997 functions as the Appellate Tribunal for the IT Act, with effect from the Finance Act 2017. It exercises all jurisdiction, powers and authority conferred on it under the IT Act.
entities: Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal

sections.title: Appeal to Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: Save as provided in sub-section (2), any person aggrieved by an order made by controller or an adjudicating officer under this Act may prefer an appeal to a Appellate Tribunal having jurisdiction in the matter.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person aggrieved by an order of the Controller or an adjudicating officer under the Act may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal, except where the order was made with the consent of the parties.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 57(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person aggrieved by an order made by the Controller or an adjudicating officer under the Act may prefer an appeal to the Appellate Tribunal having jurisdiction. No appeal lies from orders made with the consent of the parties.

sections.title: Appeal - Limitation Period
sections.clause_text: Every appeal under sub-section (1) shall be filed within a period of forty-five days from the date on which a copy of the order made by the Controller or the adjudicating officer is received by the person aggrieved and it shall be in such form and be accompanied by such fee as may be prescribed: Provided that the Appellate Tribunal may entertain an appeal after the expiry of the said period of forty-five days if it is satisfied that there was sufficient cause for not filing it within that period.
sections.plain_english_summary: Appeals must be filed within 45 days of receiving the Controller's or adjudicating officer's order, in the prescribed form with prescribed fee. The Appellate Tribunal may condone delay for sufficient cause.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 57(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. An appeal to the Appellate Tribunal must be filed within 45 days of receiving a copy of the Controller's or adjudicating officer's order, in the prescribed form and accompanied by the prescribed fee. The Appellate Tribunal may condone delay beyond the 45-day period if there was sufficient cause.

sections.title: Powers of Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: The Appellate Tribunal shall have, for the purposes of discharging its functions under this Act, the same powers as are vested in a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, while trying a suit, in respect of the following matters, namely:— (a) summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person and examining him on oath; (b) requiring the discovery and production of documents or other electronic records; (c) receiving evidence on affidavits; (d) issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses or documents; (e) reviewing its decisions; (f) dismissing an application for default or deciding it ex parte; (g) any other matter which may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Appellate Tribunal has civil court powers including summoning persons, requiring document production, receiving affidavit evidence, issuing commissions, reviewing decisions, and dismissing applications for default.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 58(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Appellate Tribunal has the powers of a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure 1908 for: summoning and examining witnesses on oath; requiring discovery and production of documents or electronic records; receiving evidence on affidavit; issuing commissions for witness or document examination; reviewing its own decisions; and dismissing applications for default or deciding ex parte.

sections.title: Civil Court - Bar on Jurisdiction
sections.clause_text: No court shall have jurisdiction to entertain any suit or proceeding in respect of any matter which an adjudicating officer appointed under this Act or the Appellate Tribunal constituted under this Act is empowered by or under this Act to determine and no injunction shall be granted by any court or other authority in respect of any action taken or to be taken in pursuance of any power conferred by or under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: Civil courts have no jurisdiction over matters within the jurisdiction of the adjudicating officer or Appellate Tribunal under this Act, and no court may grant an injunction against actions taken under the Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 61 of the Information Technology Act 2000. No civil court shall have jurisdiction to entertain any suit or proceeding in respect of any matter that the adjudicating officer or Appellate Tribunal is empowered to determine. No injunction may be granted by any court or authority in respect of any action taken or to be taken under powers conferred by the Act.

sections.title: Appeal to High Court
sections.clause_text: Any person aggrieved by any decision or order of the Appellate Tribunal may file an appeal to the High Court within sixty days from the date of communication of the decision or order of the Appellate Tribunal to him on any question of fact or law arising out of such order.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person aggrieved by an order of the Appellate Tribunal may appeal to the High Court within 60 days on any question of fact or law, with provision for condonation of delay.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 62 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person aggrieved by a decision or order of the Appellate Tribunal may file an appeal to the High Court within sixty days from the date of communication of the decision or order, on any question of fact or law. The High Court may allow an appeal filed after sixty days if the appellant demonstrates sufficient cause for the delay.

sections.title: Tampering with Computer Source Documents
sections.clause_text: Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys or alters or intentionally or knowingly causes another to conceal, destroy, or alter any computer source code used for a computer, computer programme, computer system or computer network, when the computer source code is required to be kept or maintained by law for the time being in force, shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years, or with fine which may extend up to two lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Knowingly concealing, destroying, or altering computer source code that is required to be maintained by law is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or a fine up to Rs 2 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 65 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys or alters computer source code required to be kept or maintained by law, or causes another to do so, shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years or a fine up to Rs 2 lakh or both. Computer source code means the listing of programmes, computer commands, design, layout, and programme analysis of a computer resource.

sections.title: Computer Related Offences
sections.clause_text: If any person, dishonestly or fraudulently, does any act referred to in section 43, he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person who dishonestly or fraudulently commits any of the acts listed in section 43 (such as unauthorised access, data theft, introducing viruses) is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or a fine up to Rs 5 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently commits any act described in section 43 — including unauthorised access, downloading or extracting data, introducing computer contaminants or viruses, damaging, disrupting, or denying access to computer systems — shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years or a fine up to Rs 5 lakh or both.

sections.title: Punishment for Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Computer Resource
