sections.clause_text: Whoever dishonestly receive or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen computer resource or communication device, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to rupees one lakh or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Dishonestly receiving or retaining a stolen computer resource or communication device is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66B of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device, knowing or having reason to believe it is stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
sections.clause_text: Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly make use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to rupees one lakh.
sections.plain_english_summary: Fraudulently or dishonestly using another person's electronic signature, password, or unique identification feature constitutes identity theft, punishable with imprisonment up to three years and a fine up to Rs 1 lakh.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66C of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person is guilty of identity theft and shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years and also a fine up to Rs 1 lakh.

sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation Using Computer Resource
sections.clause_text: Whoever, by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to one lakh rupees.
sections.plain_english_summary: Cheating by impersonating another person using a computer resource or communication device is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and a fine up to Rs 1 lakh.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66D of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever cheats by personation using any communication device or computer resource shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years and a fine up to Rs 1 lakh. This covers online impersonation fraud conducted through computers or mobile devices.

sections.title: Punishment for Violation of Privacy
sections.clause_text: Whoever, intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes or transmits the image of a private area of any person without his or her consent, under circumstances violating the privacy of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years or with fine not exceeding two lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Intentionally capturing, publishing, or transmitting images of a person's private areas without consent, in circumstances violating their privacy, is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or a fine up to Rs 2 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66E of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes or transmits images of a private area of any person without their consent, under circumstances violating that person's privacy, shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years or a fine not exceeding Rs 2 lakh or both. Private area means the naked or undergarment-clad genitals, pubic area, buttocks or female breast.

sections.title: Punishment for Cyber Terrorism
sections.clause_text: Whoever with intent to threaten the unity, integrity, security or sovereignty of India or to strike terror in the people by denying or causing denial of access to any authorised person, attempting to penetrate or access a computer resource without authorisation, or introducing computer contaminants, and by means of such conduct causes or is likely to cause death or injuries to persons or damage to property or disrupts supplies or services essential to the community or adversely affects the critical information infrastructure; or knowingly or intentionally penetrates or accesses a computer resource without authorisation and obtains restricted information that may be used to cause injury to the sovereignty and integrity of India, commits the offence of cyber terrorism. Whoever commits or conspires to commit cyber terrorism shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to imprisonment for life.
sections.plain_english_summary: Cyber terrorism — attacking computer systems to threaten national security, sovereignty, public order, or critical infrastructure, or obtaining restricted government information through unauthorised access — is punishable with imprisonment for life.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66F of the Information Technology Act 2000. Cyber terrorism is committed by anyone who with intent to threaten India's unity, integrity, security or sovereignty, or to strike terror, denies access to authorised users, penetrates computer resources without authorisation, or introduces contaminants causing or likely to cause death, injury, property damage, disruption of essential services, or affecting critical information infrastructure. Also covers unauthorised access to restricted State information that may harm India's sovereignty, security or foreign relations. Punishment is imprisonment for life.

sections.title: Punishment for Publishing Obscene Material in Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form, any material which is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest or if its effect is such as to tend to deprave and corrupt persons who are likely, having regard to all relevant circumstances, to read, see or hear the matter contained or embodied in it, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees and in the event of second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and also with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees.
sections.plain_english_summary: Publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine up to Rs 5 lakh on first conviction, and up to 5 years and Rs 10 lakh on subsequent conviction.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 67 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in electronic form any lascivious material or material that appeals to the prurient interest or tends to deprave and corrupt persons shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to three years and a fine up to Rs 5 lakh, and on second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment up to five years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh.

sections.title: Punishment for Publishing Sexually Explicit Material in Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form any material which contains sexually explicit act or conduct shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees and in the event of second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years and also with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees.
sections.plain_english_summary: Publishing or transmitting sexually explicit material in electronic form attracts imprisonment up to 5 years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh on first conviction, and up to 7 years and Rs 10 lakh on subsequent conviction.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 67A of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in electronic form any material containing sexually explicit acts or conduct shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment for up to five years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh, and on second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment for up to seven years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh.

sections.title: Punishment for Publishing Child Sexually Explicit Material in Electronic Form
sections.clause_text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted material in any electronic form which depicts children engaged in sexually explicit act or conduct; or creates text or digital images, collects, seeks, browses, downloads, advertises, promotes, exchanges or distributes material depicting children in obscene or indecent or sexually explicit manner; or cultivates, entices or induces children to online relationship for sexually explicit purposes; or facilitates abusing children online; or records own abuse or that of others pertaining to sexually explicit act with children, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment for a term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees and in the event of second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment which may extend to seven years and fine up to ten lakh rupees.
sections.plain_english_summary: Publishing, distributing, creating, browsing, or downloading child sexually explicit material in electronic form, or facilitating or recording child sexual abuse online, is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh on first conviction, and up to 7 years on subsequent conviction.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 67B of the Information Technology Act 2000. Offences relating to child sexually explicit material in electronic form include publishing, transmitting, creating, collecting, downloading, advertising, promoting, exchanging or distributing such material, cultivating or enticing children for online sexual relationships, facilitating child abuse online, and recording sexual abuse of children. Punishment on first conviction is imprisonment up to five years and a fine up to Rs 10 lakh; on subsequent conviction, up to seven years and Rs 10 lakh. Children means persons under 18 years.

sections.title: Preservation and Retention of Information by Intermediaries
sections.clause_text: Intermediary shall preserve and retain such information as may be specified for such duration and in such manner and format as the Central Government may prescribe. Any intermediary who intentionally or knowingly contravenes the provisions of sub-section (1) shall be punished with an imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years and also be liable to fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: Intermediaries must preserve and retain specified information for the duration, manner, and format prescribed by the Central Government. Intentional contravention is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and a fine.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 67C of the Information Technology Act 2000. Intermediaries are obligated to preserve and retain information as specified by the Central Government in terms of duration, manner and format. Intentional or knowing contravention of this preservation and retention obligation is punishable with imprisonment for up to three years and a fine.

sections.title: Power to Issue Directions for Interception, Monitoring or Decryption
sections.clause_text: Where the Central Government or a State Government or any of its officers specially authorised by the Central Government or the State Government, as the case may be, in this behalf may, if satisfied that it is necessary or expedient so to do, in the interest of the sovereignty or integrity of India, defence of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States or public order or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence relating to above or for investigation of any offence, it may subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), for reasons to be recorded in writing, by order, direct any agency of the appropriate Government to intercept, monitor or decrypt or cause to be intercepted or monitored or decrypted any information generated, transmitted, received or stored in any computer resource.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government or State Government may direct interception, monitoring, or decryption of any information in a computer resource in the interest of national security, public order, or for preventing or investigating cognizable offences, subject to safeguards and written reasons.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central or State Government may, for reasons recorded in writing, direct any government agency to intercept, monitor or decrypt information in any computer resource when necessary in the interest of the sovereignty or integrity of India, defence, State security, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, or for preventing incitement to or investigation of cognizable offences, subject to prescribed procedures and safeguards.

sections.title: Subscriber and Intermediary Obligation to Assist Interception
sections.clause_text: The subscriber or intermediary or any person in-charge of the computer resource shall, when called upon by any agency referred to in sub-section (1), extend all facilities and technical assistance to— (a) provide access to or secure access to the computer resource generating, transmitting, receiving or storing such information; or (b) intercept, monitor, or decrypt the information, as the case may be; or (c) provide information stored in computer resource.
sections.plain_english_summary: Subscribers, intermediaries, and persons in charge of computer resources must provide all technical assistance to government agencies for interception, monitoring, or decryption when directed.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Subscribers, intermediaries and persons in charge of computer resources are obligated to extend all facilities and technical assistance to government agencies directed under section 69(1), including providing or securing access to the computer resource, assisting with interception, monitoring or decryption, and providing stored information. Failure to assist is punishable with imprisonment for up to seven years and fine.

sections.title: Power to Block Public Access to Information
sections.clause_text: Where the Central Government or any of its officers specially authorised by it in this behalf is satisfied that it is necessary or expedient so to do, in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, defence of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States or public order or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence relating to above, it may subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), for reasons to be recorded in writing, by order, direct any agency of the Government or intermediary to block for access by the public or cause to be blocked for access by the public any information generated, transmitted, received, stored or hosted in any computer resource.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may direct government agencies or intermediaries to block public access to information in a computer resource in the interest of national security, public order, or to prevent cognizable offences.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69A(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may direct any government agency or intermediary to block public access to any information generated, transmitted, received, stored or hosted in a computer resource when satisfied it is necessary in the interest of sovereignty, integrity or defence of India, State security, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, or for preventing incitement to cognizable offences. Directions must be in writing with recorded reasons.

sections.title: Power to Monitor Traffic Data for Cyber Security
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, to enhance cyber security and for identification, analysis and prevention of intrusion or spread of computer contaminant in the country, by notification in the Official Gazette, authorise any agency of the Government to monitor and collect traffic data or information generated, transmitted, received or stored in any computer resource.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may authorise a government agency to monitor and collect traffic data from computer resources to enhance cyber security and prevent intrusions or computer contaminants.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69B of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may authorise a government agency by notification to monitor and collect traffic data or information in any computer resource for the purpose of enhancing cyber security, identifying and analysing intrusions, and preventing the spread of computer contaminants. Intermediaries and persons in charge of computer resources must provide technical assistance to such authorised agencies.

sections.title: Protected System - Declaration
sections.clause_text: The appropriate Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, declare any computer resource which directly or indirectly affects the facility of Critical Information Infrastructure, to be a protected system.
sections.plain_english_summary: The appropriate Government may declare any computer resource affecting Critical Information Infrastructure as a protected system by notification.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The appropriate Government may by notification declare any computer resource that directly or indirectly affects Critical Information Infrastructure to be a protected system. Critical Information Infrastructure means computer resources whose incapacitation or destruction would have a debilitating impact on national security, economy, public health or safety. Unauthorised access to a protected system is punishable with imprisonment for up to ten years.

sections.title: Protected System - Unauthorised Access Penalty
sections.clause_text: Any person who secures access or attempts to secure access to a protected system in contravention of the provisions of this section shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: Unauthorised access or attempted access to a protected system is punishable with imprisonment up to ten years and a fine.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person who secures or attempts to secure access to a protected system (a computer resource affecting Critical Information Infrastructure declared by the appropriate Government) in contravention of the Act shall be punished with imprisonment for up to ten years and a fine.

sections.title: National Nodal Agency for Critical Information Infrastructure Protection
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, by notification published in the Official Gazette, designate any organisation of the Government as the national nodal agency in respect of Critical Information Infrastructure Protection. The national nodal agency designated under sub-section (1) shall be responsible for all measures including Research and Development relating to protection of Critical Information Infrastructure.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may designate a government organisation as the national nodal agency for Critical Information Infrastructure Protection, responsible for all protection measures including R&D.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70A of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may designate any government organisation as the national nodal agency for Critical Information Infrastructure Protection. That agency is responsible for all protection measures, including research and development, relating to Critical Information Infrastructure.

sections.title: Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In)
sections.clause_text: The Central Government shall, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint an agency of the Government to be called the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team. The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team shall serve as the national agency for performing the following functions in the area of cyber security,— (a) collection, analysis and dissemination of information on cyber incidents; (b) forecast and alerts of cyber security incidents; (c) emergency measures for handling cyber security incidents; (d) coordination of cyber incidents response activities; (e) issue guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and white papers relating to information security practices, procedures, prevention, response and reporting of cyber incidents; (f) such other functions relating to cyber security as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government establishes CERT-In as the national cyber security agency responsible for collecting and analysing cyber incident information, issuing alerts, handling emergencies, coordinating responses, and issuing cyber security guidelines.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70B of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government establishes the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) as the national agency for cyber security. CERT-In's functions include collection, analysis and dissemination of cyber incident information, issuing forecasts and alerts, emergency response measures, coordinating cyber incident response, and issuing guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and white papers on information security practices. Service providers, intermediaries, data centres and body corporates must comply with CERT-In directions.

sections.title: Penalty for Misrepresentation to Controller or Certifying Authority
sections.clause_text: Whoever makes any misrepresentation to, or suppresses any material fact from the Controller or the Certifying Authority for obtaining any licence or electronic signature Certificate, as the case may be, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Making a misrepresentation or suppressing material facts when obtaining a licence or electronic signature certificate from the Controller or Certifying Authority is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 71 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever makes any misrepresentation to, or suppresses any material fact from, the Controller or a Certifying Authority for the purpose of obtaining a licence or electronic signature certificate shall be punished with imprisonment for up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Penalty for Breach of Confidentiality and Privacy
sections.clause_text: Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, if any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred under this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material to any other person shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person who, while exercising powers under the Act, obtains access to electronic records or information without the concerned person's consent and then discloses it to another person is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 72 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person who, while exercising powers conferred under the Act, secures access to electronic records, books, registers, correspondence, information, documents or other material without the consent of the person concerned and discloses such material to any other person shall be punished with imprisonment for up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both. This protects confidentiality of information accessed in exercise of official powers.

sections.title: Punishment for Disclosure of Information in Breach of Lawful Contract
sections.clause_text: Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, any person including an intermediary who, while providing services under the terms of lawful contract, has secured access to any material containing personal information about another person, with the intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or wrongful gain discloses, without the consent of the person concerned, or in breach of a lawful contract, such material to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any person, including an intermediary, who discloses personal information accessed under a lawful contract without consent and with intent to cause wrongful loss or gain is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or a fine up to Rs 5 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 72A of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any person including an intermediary who, while providing services under a lawful contract, accesses material containing personal information about another person and discloses it without consent or in breach of the contract, with intent to cause or knowing it is likely to cause wrongful loss or wrongful gain, shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years or a fine up to Rs 5 lakh or both. This protects personal information shared with service providers and intermediaries.

sections.title: Extra-Territorial Application for Offences Outside India
sections.clause_text: Subject to the provisions of sub-section (2), the provisions of this Act shall apply also to any offence or contravention committed outside India by any person irrespective of his nationality. For the purposes of sub-section (1), this Act shall apply to an offence or contravention committed outside India by any person if the act or conduct constituting the offence or contravention involves a computer, computer system or computer network located in India.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Act applies to offences committed outside India by any person, regardless of nationality, if the act involves a computer, computer system, or computer network located in India.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 75 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Act has extra-territorial application and applies to any offence or contravention committed outside India by any person irrespective of nationality, provided the act involves a computer, computer system or computer network located in India.

sections.title: Safe Harbour - Intermediary Exemption from Liability
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force but subject to the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3), an intermediary shall not be liable for any third party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by him.
sections.plain_english_summary: An intermediary is not liable for third-party information, data, or communication links it makes available or hosts, subject to conditions in subsections (2) and (3).
sections.retrieval_text: Section 79(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. An intermediary shall not be liable for any third-party information, data or communication link made available or hosted by it, subject to compliance with the conditions set out in sections 79(2) and 79(3). This creates a conditional safe harbour from liability for intermediaries acting as mere conduits.

sections.title: Safe Harbour - Conditions for Exemption
sections.clause_text: The provisions of sub-section (1) shall apply if— (a) the function of the intermediary is limited to providing access to a communication system over which information made available by third parties is transmitted or temporarily stored or hosted; or (b) the intermediary does not— (i) initiate the transmission, (ii) select the receiver of the transmission, and (iii) select or modify the information contained in the transmission; (c) the intermediary observes due diligence while discharging his duties under this Act and also observes such other guidelines as the Central Government may prescribe in this behalf.
sections.plain_english_summary: An intermediary qualifies for safe harbour if it acts only as a conduit, does not initiate or modify transmissions, does not select recipients, and observes due diligence and prescribed guidelines.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 79(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The safe harbour from liability under section 79(1) applies if the intermediary's function is limited to providing access to a communication system for third-party information, it does not initiate the transmission, select the receiver, or select or modify the content, and it observes due diligence and guidelines prescribed by the Central Government.

sections.title: Safe Harbour - When Exemption Ceases
sections.clause_text: The provisions of sub-section (1) shall not apply if— (a) the intermediary has conspired or abetted or aided or induced, whether by threats or promise or otherwise in the commission of the unlawful act; (b) upon receiving actual knowledge, or on being notified by the appropriate Government or its agency that any information, data or communication link residing in or connected to a computer resource controlled by the intermediary is being used to commit the unlawful act, the intermediary fails to expeditiously remove or disable access to that material on that resource without vitiating the evidence in any manner.
sections.plain_english_summary: The safe harbour does not apply if the intermediary conspired in or induced the unlawful act, or if, after receiving actual knowledge or government notification that content is being used unlawfully, the intermediary fails to expeditiously remove or disable access to that content.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 79(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. An intermediary loses safe harbour protection if it conspires in, abets, aids or induces the commission of an unlawful act, or if, upon receiving actual knowledge or government notification that information on its platform is being used to commit an unlawful act, it fails to expeditiously remove or disable access to that material without vitiating evidence.

sections.title: Examiner of Electronic Evidence
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of providing expert opinion on electronic form evidence before any court or other authority specify, by notification in the Official Gazette, any Department, body or agency of the Central Government or a State Government as an Examiner of Electronic Evidence.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may designate a government department, body, or agency as an Examiner of Electronic Evidence to provide expert opinions on electronic evidence before courts or other authorities.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 79A of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may designate, by notification, any Central or State Government department, body or agency as an Examiner of Electronic Evidence for the purpose of providing expert opinions on electronic evidence before courts or other authorities. Electronic form evidence includes computer evidence, digital audio, digital video, cell phones and digital fax machines.

sections.title: Power of Police Officer to Enter, Search and Arrest
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, any police officer, not below the rank of a Inspector, or any other officer of the Central Government or a State Government authorised by the Central Government in this behalf may enter any public place and search and arrest without warrant any person found therein who is reasonably suspected of having committed or of committing or of being about to commit any offence under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: A police officer of Inspector rank or above, or an authorised Central or State Government officer, may enter any public place and arrest without warrant any person reasonably suspected of committing an offence under the Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 80 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A police officer not below the rank of Inspector, or an authorised Central or State Government officer, may enter any public place and search and arrest without warrant any person reasonably suspected of having committed, committing, or being about to commit an offence under the Act. This power operates notwithstanding the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973.

sections.title: Overriding Effect of the Act
sections.clause_text: The provisions of this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. Provided that nothing contained in this Act shall restrict any person from exercising any right conferred under the Copyright Act, 1957 or the Patents Act, 1970.
sections.plain_english_summary: This Act overrides any inconsistent provisions in other laws, but does not restrict rights under the Copyright Act, 1957 or the Patents Act, 1970.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 81 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Act has overriding effect and prevails over any inconsistent provision in any other law currently in force. However, this override does not restrict any person from exercising rights conferred by the Copyright Act 1957 or the Patents Act 1970.

sections.title: Protection of Action Taken in Good Faith
sections.clause_text: No suit, prosecution or other legal proceeding shall lie against the Central Government, the State Government, the Controller or any person acting on behalf of him, and adjudicating officers for anything which is in good faith done or intended to be done in pursuance of this Act or any rule, regulation or order made thereunder.
sections.plain_english_summary: No legal action lies against the Central Government, State Government, Controller, adjudicating officers, or persons acting on their behalf for anything done in good faith under the Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 84 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government, State Government, the Controller, adjudicating officers, and any person acting on behalf of the Controller are protected from suits, prosecutions or other legal proceedings for anything done or intended to be done in good faith in pursuance of the Act or rules, regulations or orders made thereunder.

sections.title: Modes or Methods for Encryption
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, for secure use of the electronic medium and for promotion of e-governance and e-commerce, prescribe the modes or methods for encryption.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may prescribe modes or methods of encryption to promote secure electronic communication, e-governance, and e-commerce.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 84A of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government is empowered to prescribe modes or methods for encryption for the purpose of ensuring secure use of the electronic medium and promoting e-governance and e-commerce.

sections.title: Punishment for Abetment of Offences
sections.clause_text: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Act for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: Whoever abets an offence under the Act, where the abetted act is actually committed and no separate abetment penalty is provided, shall be punished with the same punishment as the principal offence.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 84B of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever abets any offence under the Act is, if the abetted act is committed in consequence of the abetment and no express provision is made for the punishment of such abetment, punishable with the same punishment provided for the principal offence.

sections.title: Punishment for Attempt to Commit Offences
sections.clause_text: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Act or causes such an offence to be committed, and in such an attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Attempting to commit an offence under the Act carries a maximum penalty of half the term of imprisonment or fine prescribed for the completed offence, where no separate provision exists for the attempt.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 84C of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever attempts to commit an offence under the Act and does any act towards its commission shall, where no express provision covers such attempt, be punished with imprisonment for up to half the longest term provided for the offence, or with the fine provided, or both.

sections.title: Offences by Companies - Corporate Liability
sections.clause_text: Where a person committing a contravention of any of the provisions of this Act or of any rule, direction or order made thereunder is a company, every person who, at the time the contravention was committed, was in charge of, and was responsible to, the company for the conduct of business of the company as well as the company, shall be guilty of the contravention and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly. Notwithstanding the foregoing, where a contravention has been committed by a company and it is proved that the contravention has taken place with the consent or connivance of, or is attributable to any neglect on the part of, any director, manager, secretary or other officer of the company, such director, manager, secretary or other officer shall also be deemed to be guilty.
sections.plain_english_summary: When a company violates the Act, both the company and persons in charge of its conduct are liable. Directors, managers, secretaries, or other officers who consent, connive in, or neglect to prevent the contravention are also personally liable.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 85 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Corporate liability: where a company commits a contravention, every person in charge of the company's conduct at the time of the contravention is guilty and liable along with the company, unless they prove the contravention occurred without their knowledge and despite due diligence. Directors, managers, secretaries or other officers who consent, connive in, or whose neglect causes a contravention are also personally liable.

sections.title: Rule-Making Powers of Central Government
sections.clause_text: In particular, and without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing power, such rules may provide for all or any of the following matters, namely: the conditions for reliability of electronic signature; manner of authentication; electronic form for government filings; manner of service charges for service providers; type, manner and format of electronic signature; security procedures and practices; qualifications of adjudicating officers; information retention and preservation under section 67C; procedures for interception, monitoring or decryption under sections 69, 69A, 69B; information security for protected systems; functions of CERT-In; and guidelines for intermediaries under section 79.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may make rules covering electronic signature reliability, authentication procedures, e-governance filings, security practices, adjudication procedures, data retention by intermediaries, surveillance safeguards, and intermediary guidelines, among other matters.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 87(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government's rule-making power covers: reliability conditions for electronic signatures; authentication procedures; electronic form for government filings; service provider charges; electronic signature type, manner and format; security procedures; adjudicating officer qualifications; information retention by intermediaries under section 67C; procedures and safeguards for interception, monitoring and decryption under sections 69, 69A, 69B; information security practices for protected systems under section 70; functions of CERT-In under section 70B; and guidelines for intermediaries under section 79.

sections.title: Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee
sections.clause_text: The Central Government shall, as soon as may be after the commencement of this Act, constitute a Committee called the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee. The Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee shall consist of a Chairperson and such number of other official and non-official members representing the interests principally affected or having special knowledge of the subject-matter as the Central Government may deem fit. The Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee shall advise— (a) the Central Government either generally as regards any rules or for any other purpose connected with this Act; (b) the Controller in framing the regulations under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government must constitute the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee to advise the Central Government on rules under the Act and the Controller on framing regulations.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 88 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government is required to constitute the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee, consisting of a Chairperson and official and non-official members with relevant expertise. The Committee advises the Central Government on rules under the Act and the Controller in framing regulations.

sections.title: Documents or Transactions Excluded from the Act
sections.clause_text: Documents or transactions to which the Act shall not apply: 1. A negotiable instrument (other than a cheque) as defined in section 13 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. 2. A power-of-attorney as defined in section 1A of the Powers-of-Attorney Act, 1882. 3. A trust as defined in section 3 of the Indian Trust Act, 1882. 4. A will as defined in clause (h) of section 2 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925, including any other testamentary disposition by whatever name called. 5. Any contract for the sale or conveyance of immovable property or any interest in such property.
sections.plain_english_summary: The First Schedule lists documents and transactions excluded from the Act: negotiable instruments (other than cheques), powers of attorney, trusts, wills and testamentary dispositions, and contracts for sale or conveyance of immovable property.
sections.retrieval_text: First Schedule to the Information Technology Act 2000. Lists documents and transactions to which the Act does not apply: negotiable instruments (excluding cheques) under the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881; powers of attorney under the Powers-of-Attorney Act 1882; trusts under the Indian Trust Act 1882; wills and testamentary dispositions under the Indian Succession Act 1925; and contracts for the sale or conveyance of immovable property or any interest therein.

sections.title: Compounding of Offences
sections.clause_text: A court of competent jurisdiction may compound offences, other than offences for which the punishment for life or imprisonment for a term exceeding three years has been provided, under this Act: Provided that the court shall not compound such offence where the accused is, by reason of his previous conviction, liable to either enhanced punishment or to a punishment of a different kind: Provided further that the court shall not compound any offence where such offence affects the socio economic conditions of the country or has been committed against a child below the age of 18 years or a woman.
sections.plain_english_summary: A court may compound offences under the Act except those carrying life imprisonment or imprisonment exceeding three years, or where the accused has a prior conviction attracting enhanced punishment, or the offence affects socio-economic conditions or is against a child or woman.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 77A of the Information Technology Act 2000. Courts of competent jurisdiction may compound IT Act offences, except those punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment exceeding three years. Compounding is not permitted where the accused faces enhanced punishment due to prior conviction, or where the offence affects the socio-economic conditions of the country or was committed against a child below 18 years or a woman.

sections.title: Bailable and Cognizable Offences
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, the offence punishable with imprisonment of three years and above shall be cognizable and the offence punishable with imprisonment of three years shall be bailable.
sections.plain_english_summary: Offences punishable with imprisonment of three years or more are cognizable. Offences punishable with exactly three years are bailable, notwithstanding the CrPC.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 77B of the Information Technology Act 2000. Notwithstanding the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973, offences under the Act punishable with imprisonment of three years and above are cognizable (police can arrest without warrant), and offences punishable with imprisonment of three years are bailable.

sections.title: Power to Investigate Offences
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, a police officer not below the rank of Inspector shall investigate any offence under this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: Only a police officer of Inspector rank or above may investigate offences under the Act, notwithstanding the CrPC.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 78 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Notwithstanding the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973, only a police officer of Inspector rank or above may investigate any offence under the IT Act. This ensures cyber offences are investigated by officers with appropriate rank and expertise.

sections.title: Compounding of Contraventions
sections.clause_text: Any contravention under this Act may, either before or after the institution of adjudication proceedings, be compounded by the Controller or such other officer as may be specially authorised by him in this behalf or by the adjudicating officer, as the case may be, subject to such conditions as the Controller or such other officer or the adjudicating officer may specify: Provided that such sum shall not, in any case, exceed the maximum amount of the penalty which may be imposed under this Act for the contravention so compounded.
sections.plain_english_summary: Contraventions under the Act may be compounded by the Controller or adjudicating officer before or after adjudication proceedings begin, subject to conditions and a cap at the maximum penalty for the contravention. A person cannot have a contravention compounded twice within three years.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 63 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any contravention under the Act may be compounded by the Controller or adjudicating officer, before or after adjudication proceedings are instituted, subject to conditions and a cap not exceeding the maximum penalty for that contravention. A person who commits the same or similar contravention within three years of a previous compounded contravention cannot benefit from compounding again.

sections.title: Recovery of Penalty or Compensation
sections.clause_text: A penalty imposed or compensation awarded under this Act, if it is not paid, shall be recovered as an arrear of land revenue and the licence or the electronic signature Certificate, as the case may be, shall be suspended till the penalty is paid.
sections.plain_english_summary: Unpaid penalties or compensation under the Act are recoverable as arrears of land revenue, and the relevant licence or electronic signature certificate is suspended until payment is made.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 64 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any penalty imposed or compensation awarded under the Act that remains unpaid shall be recovered as an arrear of land revenue. Additionally, the relevant licence or electronic signature certificate shall be suspended until the outstanding penalty or compensation is paid.

sections.title: Definition - Affixing Electronic Signature
sections.clause_text: "affixing electronic signature" with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions means adoption of any methodology or procedure by a person for the purpose of authenticating an electronic record by means of digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: Affixing an electronic signature means adopting any method or procedure to authenticate an electronic record using a digital signature.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(d) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines affixing electronic signature as the adoption of any methodology or procedure by a person to authenticate an electronic record by means of digital signature.

sections.title: Definition - Appropriate Government
sections.clause_text: "appropriate Government" means as respects any matter,— (i) enumerated in List II of the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution; (ii) relating to any State law enacted under List III of the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution, the State Government and in any other case, the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Appropriate Government means the State Government for matters under List II (State List) or List III (Concurrent List) of the Constitution, and the Central Government in all other cases.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(e) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines appropriate Government as the State Government for matters in List II (State List) or List III (Concurrent List) of the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution, and the Central Government for all other matters.

sections.title: Definition - Asymmetric Crypto System
sections.clause_text: "asymmetric crypto system" means a system of a secure key pair consisting of a private key for creating a digital signature and a public key to verify the digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: An asymmetric crypto system is a cryptographic key pair where a private key creates a digital signature and the corresponding public key verifies it.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(f) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines asymmetric crypto system as a system of a secure key pair consisting of a private key for creating a digital signature and a public key to verify that digital signature. This underpins the digital signature framework under section 3.

sections.title: Definition - Certification Practice Statement
sections.clause_text: "certification practice statement" means a statement issued by a Certifying Authority to specify the practices that the Certifying Authority employs in issuing electronic signature Certificates.
sections.plain_english_summary: A certification practice statement is a document issued by a Certifying Authority setting out the practices it uses when issuing electronic signature certificates.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(h) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines certification practice statement as a statement issued by a Certifying Authority specifying the practices it employs in issuing electronic signature certificates. It is a required document accompanying licence applications and certificate issuance under sections 22 and 35.

sections.title: Definition - Computer System
sections.clause_text: "computer system" means a device or collection of devices, including input and output support devices and excluding calculators which are not programmable and capable of being used in conjunction with external files, which contain computer programmes, electronic instructions, input data and output data, that performs logic, arithmetic, data storage and retrieval, communication control and other functions.
sections.plain_english_summary: A computer system is a programmable device or collection of devices that processes logic, arithmetic, data storage, retrieval, and communication functions, including input/output devices but excluding non-programmable calculators.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(l) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines computer system as a device or collection of devices containing computer programmes, electronic instructions, and data that performs logic, arithmetic, data storage and retrieval, and communication functions. Non-programmable calculators are excluded.

sections.title: Definition - Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: "Digital Signature Certificate" means a Digital Signature Certificate issued under sub-section (4) of section 35.
sections.plain_english_summary: A Digital Signature Certificate is a certificate issued by a Certifying Authority under section 35(4) that validates the subscriber's public key.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(q) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines Digital Signature Certificate as a certificate issued under section 35(4) by a Certifying Authority. It certifies the subscriber's public key and its relationship with the private key used to create digital signatures.

sections.title: Definition - Electronic Gazette
sections.clause_text: "Electronic Gazette" means the Official Gazette published in the electronic form.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Electronic Gazette is the Official Gazette published in electronic form.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(s) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines Electronic Gazette as the Official Gazette published in electronic form. Under section 8, publication in the Electronic Gazette satisfies any legal requirement for publication in the Official Gazette.

sections.title: Definition - Electronic Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: "Electronic Signature Certificate" means an Electronic Signature Certificate issued under section 35 and includes Digital Signature Certificate.
sections.plain_english_summary: An Electronic Signature Certificate is a certificate issued under section 35 that includes Digital Signature Certificates.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(tb) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines Electronic Signature Certificate as any certificate issued under section 35, including a Digital Signature Certificate. It is issued by a Certifying Authority to certify the subscriber's electronic signature authentication credentials.

sections.title: Definition - Key Pair
sections.clause_text: "key pair", in an asymmetric crypto system, means a private key and its mathematically related public key, which are so related that the public key can verify a digital signature created by the private key.
sections.plain_english_summary: A key pair consists of a mathematically related private key and public key, where the public key can verify digital signatures created by the private key.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(x) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines key pair in an asymmetric crypto system as a private key and its mathematically related public key, structured so that the public key can verify any digital signature created by the corresponding private key.

sections.title: Definition - Private Key
sections.clause_text: "private key" means the key of a key pair used to create a digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: A private key is the key in a key pair used to create a digital signature.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(zc) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines private key as the key of a key pair that is used to create a digital signature. The private key must be kept under the exclusive control of the subscriber and its compromise must be reported to the Certifying Authority.

sections.title: Definition - Public Key
sections.clause_text: "public key" means the key of a key pair used to verify a digital signature and listed in the Digital Signature Certificate.
sections.plain_english_summary: A public key is the key in a key pair used to verify a digital signature, and it is listed in the Digital Signature Certificate.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(zd) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines public key as the key of a key pair used to verify a digital signature, which is listed in the subscriber's Digital Signature Certificate. Any person may use the public key to verify digital signatures created by the corresponding private key.

sections.title: Definition - Security Procedure
sections.clause_text: "security procedure" means the security procedure prescribed under section 16 by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: Security procedure means the procedure prescribed by the Central Government under section 16 for securing electronic records and signatures.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(zf) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines security procedure as the procedure prescribed by the Central Government under section 16. These procedures govern what constitutes a secure electronic record and a secure electronic signature.

sections.title: Definition - Verify
sections.clause_text: "verify", in relation to a digital signature, electronic record or public key, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, means to determine whether— (a) the initial electronic record was affixed with the digital signature by the use of private key corresponding to the public key of the subscriber; (b) the initial electronic record is retained intact or has been altered since such electronic record was so affixed with the digital signature.
sections.plain_english_summary: To verify a digital signature means to determine that the electronic record was signed using the subscriber's private key and that the record has not been altered since it was signed.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(1)(zh) of the Information Technology Act 2000 defines verify as the process of determining, in relation to a digital signature, electronic record or public key, whether the initial electronic record was affixed with a digital signature using the private key corresponding to the subscriber's public key, and whether the record remains intact or has been altered since signing.

sections.title: Cross-Reference Interpretation
sections.clause_text: Any reference in this Act to any enactment or any provision thereof shall, in relation to an area in which such enactment or such provision is not in force, be construed as a reference to the corresponding law or the relevant provision of the corresponding law, if any, in force in that area.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any reference in the Act to a law or provision not in force in a particular area shall be read as a reference to the corresponding law or provision that is in force in that area.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 2(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where the Act refers to any enactment or provision that is not in force in a particular area, that reference shall be construed as a reference to the corresponding law or provision in force in that area. This ensures the Act operates uniformly across all territories of India regardless of local variations in applicable law.

sections.title: Use of Electronic Records in Government - Rule-Making
sections.clause_text: The appropriate Government may, for the purposes of sub-section (1), by rules, prescribe— (a) the manner and format in which such electronic records shall be filed, created or issued; (b) the manner or method of payment of any fee or charges for filing, creation or issue any electronic record under clause (a).
sections.plain_english_summary: The appropriate Government may make rules prescribing the manner, format, and method of payment for electronic filing, creation, or issuance of records under section 6(1).
sections.retrieval_text: Section 6(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The appropriate Government may make rules to prescribe the manner and format for filing, creating or issuing electronic records with Government, and the manner or method of payment of fees or charges for such electronic filings under section 6(1).

sections.title: Power to Make Rules on Electronic Signature
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may, for the purposes of this Act, by rules, prescribe— (a) the type of electronic signature; (b) the manner and format in which the electronic signature shall be affixed; (c) the manner or procedure which facilitates identification of the person affixing the electronic signature; (d) control processes and procedures to ensure adequate integrity, security and confidentiality of electronic records or payments; and (e) any other matter which is necessary to give legal effect to electronic signatures.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may make rules covering the type, manner, and format of electronic signatures, procedures for identifying the signatory, and controls to ensure integrity and security of electronic records.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 10 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may make rules prescribing the type of electronic signature, the manner and format in which it shall be affixed, procedures for identifying the signatory, and control processes to ensure integrity, security and confidentiality of electronic records or payments, and any other matter necessary to give legal effect to electronic signatures.

sections.title: Acknowledgment - No Stipulation and Reasonable Time
sections.clause_text: Where the originator has not stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of such acknowledgment, and the acknowledgment has not been received by the originator within the time specified or agreed or, if no time has been specified or agreed to within a reasonable time, then the originator may give notice to the addressee stating that no acknowledgment has been received by him and specifying a reasonable time by which the acknowledgment must be received by him and if no acknowledgment is received within the aforesaid time limit he may after giving notice to the addressee, treat the electronic record as though it has never been sent.
sections.plain_english_summary: Where the originator has not made binding effect conditional on acknowledgment but none has arrived within the stipulated or reasonable time, the originator may give notice and, if still no acknowledgment is received, treat the record as never sent.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 12(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where the originator has not made the electronic record's binding effect conditional on acknowledgment, but acknowledgment has not been received within the specified or reasonable time, the originator may give notice specifying a reasonable deadline. If acknowledgment is still not received by that deadline, the originator may treat the electronic record as though it was never sent.

sections.title: Place of Despatch and Receipt of Electronic Record
sections.clause_text: Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, an electronic record is deemed to be despatched at the place where the originator has his place of business, and is deemed to be received at the place where the addressee has his place of business.
sections.plain_english_summary: Unless agreed otherwise, an electronic record is deemed dispatched from the originator's place of business and received at the addressee's place of business.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 13(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Unless otherwise agreed, an electronic record is deemed to be dispatched at the originator's place of business and received at the addressee's place of business, regardless of where the computer resource processing the record is located.

sections.title: Application for Certifying Authority Licence
sections.clause_text: Every application for issue of a licence shall be in such form as may be prescribed by the Central Government. Every application for issue of a licence shall be accompanied by— (a) a certification practice statement; (b) a statement including the procedures with respect to identification of the applicant; (c) payment of such fees, not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees as may be prescribed by the Central Government; (d) such other documents, as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.plain_english_summary: An application for a Certifying Authority licence must be in the prescribed form, accompanied by a certification practice statement, identification procedures, a fee not exceeding Rs 25,000, and other prescribed documents.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 22 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every application for a Certifying Authority licence must be in the prescribed form and accompanied by a certification practice statement, a statement of identification procedures, payment of prescribed fees not exceeding Rs 25,000, and other prescribed documents.

sections.title: Renewal of Certifying Authority Licence
sections.clause_text: An application for renewal of a licence shall be— (a) in such form; (b) accompanied by such fees, not exceeding five thousand rupees, as may be prescribed by the Central Government and shall be made not less than forty-five days before the date of expiry of the period of validity of the licence.
sections.plain_english_summary: A licence renewal application must be submitted at least 45 days before expiry, in the prescribed form, with a fee not exceeding Rs 5,000.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 23 of the Information Technology Act 2000. An application for renewal of a Certifying Authority licence must be made in the prescribed form at least 45 days before expiry, accompanied by prescribed fees not exceeding Rs 5,000.

sections.title: Procedure for Grant or Rejection of Licence
sections.clause_text: The Controller may, on receipt of an application under sub-section (1) of section 21, after considering the documents accompanying the application and such other factors, as he deems fit, grant the licence or reject the application: Provided that no application shall be rejected under this section unless the applicant has been given a reasonable opportunity of presenting his case.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may grant or reject a Certifying Authority licence after considering accompanying documents and other relevant factors, but must give the applicant a reasonable opportunity to present their case before rejection.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 24 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may grant or reject a licence application under section 21 after considering the accompanying documents and such other factors as deemed fit. No application may be rejected without first giving the applicant a reasonable opportunity to present their case.

sections.title: Notice of Suspension or Revocation of Certifying Authority Licence
sections.clause_text: Where the licence of the Certifying Authority is suspended or revoked, the Controller shall publish notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in the data base maintained by him. Where one or more repositories are specified, the Controller shall publish notices of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in all such repositories: Provided that the data base containing the notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, shall be made available through a web site which shall be accessible round the clock.
sections.plain_english_summary: When a Certifying Authority licence is suspended or revoked, the Controller must publish notice in the relevant database and all specified repositories, accessible round the clock via a website.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 26 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller is obligated to publish notice of any suspension or revocation of a Certifying Authority licence in the Controller's database and all specified repositories. The database must be accessible round the clock through a website.

sections.title: Power to Delegate
sections.clause_text: The Controller may, in writing, authorise the Deputy Controller, Assistant Controller or any officer to exercise any of the powers of the Controller under this Chapter.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may delegate any of their powers under Chapter VI to the Deputy Controller, Assistant Controller, or any other officer, by written authorisation.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 27 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may delegate any powers under Chapter VI (Regulation of Certifying Authorities) to the Deputy Controller, Assistant Controller, or any other officer by written authorisation.

sections.title: Power to Investigate Contraventions
sections.clause_text: The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall take up for investigation any contravention of the provisions of this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder. The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall exercise the like powers which are conferred on Income-tax authorities under Chapter XIII of the Income-tax Act, 1961, and shall exercise such powers, subject to such limitations laid down under that Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller or an authorised officer may investigate contraventions of the Act and its rules, with powers equivalent to Income Tax authorities under Chapter XIII of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 28 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller or any authorised officer may investigate contraventions of the Act and rules and regulations thereunder. For investigations, they have the same powers as Income Tax authorities under Chapter XIII of the Income Tax Act 1961, subject to the limitations of that Act.

sections.title: Access to Computers and Data for Investigation
sections.clause_text: Without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 69, the Controller or any person authorised by him shall, if he has reasonable cause to suspect that any contravention of the provisions of this Chapter has been committed, have access to any computer system, any apparatus, data or any other material connected with such system, for the purpose of searching or causing a search to be made for obtaining any information or data contained in or available to such computer system.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller or an authorised person may access any computer system and data when there is reasonable cause to suspect a contravention of the Act, for the purpose of searching for relevant information.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 29 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller or any authorised person may, on reasonable suspicion of a contravention of Chapter VI, access any computer system, apparatus, data or related material for the purpose of searching for information or data. They may also direct persons in charge of such systems to provide technical assistance.

sections.title: Certifying Authority - Display of Licence
sections.clause_text: Every Certifying Authority shall display its licence at a conspicuous place of the premises in which it carries on its business.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every Certifying Authority must display its licence at a conspicuous place in its business premises.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 32 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every Certifying Authority is obligated to display its licence at a conspicuous place in the premises where it carries on its business.

sections.title: Certifying Authority - Surrender of Licence
sections.clause_text: Every Certifying Authority whose licence is suspended or revoked shall immediately after such suspension or revocation, surrender the licence to the Controller. Where any Certifying Authority fails to surrender a licence under sub-section (1), the person in whose favour a licence is issued, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend up to six months or a fine which may extend up to ten thousand rupees or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: A Certifying Authority whose licence is suspended or revoked must immediately surrender the licence to the Controller. Failure to do so is an offence punishable with imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 10,000, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 33 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A Certifying Authority whose licence is suspended or revoked must immediately surrender the licence to the Controller. Failure to surrender is an offence punishable with imprisonment for up to six months or a fine up to Rs 10,000 or both.

sections.title: Representations Upon Issuance of Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: A Certifying Authority while issuing a Digital Signature Certificate shall certify that— (a) it has complied with the provisions of this Act and the rules and regulations made thereunder; (b) it has published the Digital Signature Certificate or otherwise made it available to such person relying on it and the subscriber has accepted it; (c) the subscriber holds the private key corresponding to the public key, listed in the Digital Signature Certificate; (ca) the subscriber holds a private key which is capable of creating a digital signature; (cb) the public key to be listed in the certificate can be used to verify a digital signature affixed by the private key held by the subscriber; (d) the subscriber's public key and private key constitute a functioning key pair; (e) the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate is accurate; and (f) it has no knowledge of any material fact, which if included in the Digital Signature Certificate would adversely affect the reliability of the representations.
sections.plain_english_summary: When issuing a Digital Signature Certificate, a Certifying Authority must certify compliance with the Act, accuracy of information, that the subscriber holds the correct private key capable of creating digital signatures, that the key pair functions correctly, and absence of knowledge of any adverse material fact.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 36 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A Certifying Authority issuing a Digital Signature Certificate must certify: its own compliance with the Act; publication and subscriber acceptance of the certificate; that the subscriber holds the corresponding private key capable of creating digital signatures; that the public key can verify those signatures; that the key pair is functional; that the certificate information is accurate; and that no adverse material fact is known.

sections.title: Notice of Suspension or Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: Where a Digital Signature Certificate is suspended or revoked under section 37 or section 38, the Certifying Authority shall publish a notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in the repository specified in the Digital Signature Certificate for publication of such notice. Where one or more repositories are specified, the Certifying Authority shall publish notices of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in all such repositories.
sections.plain_english_summary: When a Digital Signature Certificate is suspended or revoked, the Certifying Authority must publish notice of the suspension or revocation in all repositories specified in the certificate.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 39 of the Information Technology Act 2000. When a Digital Signature Certificate is suspended under section 37 or revoked under section 38, the Certifying Authority must promptly publish notice of the suspension or revocation in the repository or repositories specified in the certificate.

sections.title: Duties of Subscriber of Electronic Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: In respect of Electronic Signature Certificate the subscriber shall perform such duties as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: A subscriber holding an Electronic Signature Certificate must perform such duties as are prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 40A of the Information Technology Act 2000. A subscriber of an Electronic Signature Certificate must perform such duties as are prescribed. This provision enables the Central Government to prescribe specific obligations on subscribers of electronic signature certificates beyond those relating to Digital Signature Certificates.

sections.title: Acceptance of Digital Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: A subscriber shall be deemed to have accepted a Digital Signature Certificate if he publishes or authorises the publication of a Digital Signature Certificate— (a) to one or more persons; (b) in a repository; or otherwise demonstrates his approval of the Digital Signature Certificate in any manner. By accepting a Digital Signature Certificate the subscriber certifies to all who reasonably rely on the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate that— (a) the subscriber holds the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate and is entitled to hold the same; (b) all representations made by the subscriber to the Certifying Authority and all material relevant to the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate are true; (c) all information in the Digital Signature Certificate that is within the knowledge of the subscriber is true.
sections.plain_english_summary: A subscriber is deemed to have accepted a Digital Signature Certificate by publishing it or otherwise demonstrating approval, and in doing so certifies the accuracy of all information in it to anyone relying on it.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 41 of the Information Technology Act 2000. A subscriber who publishes, authorises publication of, or otherwise demonstrates approval of a Digital Signature Certificate is deemed to have accepted it. By accepting, the subscriber certifies to relying parties that the private key information is accurate, all representations to the Certifying Authority are true, and all certificate information within the subscriber's knowledge is accurate.

sections.title: Adjudication - Inquiry and Imposition of Penalty
