sections.clause_text: The adjudicating officer shall, after giving the person referred to in sub-section (1) a reasonable opportunity for making representation in the matter and if, on such inquiry, he is satisfied that the person has committed the contravention, he may impose such penalty or award such compensation as he thinks fit in accordance with the provisions of that section.
sections.plain_english_summary: The adjudicating officer must give the accused a reasonable opportunity to make representations before imposing any penalty or awarding compensation.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 46(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The adjudicating officer must provide the person accused of a contravention a reasonable opportunity to make representations before concluding an inquiry. If satisfied the contravention occurred, the adjudicating officer may impose such penalty or award such compensation as appropriate.

sections.title: Adjudicating Officer - Civil Court Powers
sections.clause_text: Every adjudicating officer shall have the powers of a civil court which are conferred on the Appellate Tribunal under sub-section (2) of section 58, and— (a) all proceedings before it shall be deemed to be judicial proceedings within the meaning of sections 193 and 228 of the Indian Penal Code; (b) shall be deemed to be a civil court for the purposes of sections 345 and 346 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973; (c) shall be deemed to be a civil court for purposes of Order XXI of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908.
sections.plain_english_summary: An adjudicating officer has civil court powers including those under the CPC, and proceedings before them are deemed judicial proceedings under the IPC and CrPC.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 46(5) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Every adjudicating officer has the civil court powers conferred on the Appellate Tribunal under section 58(2). Proceedings before adjudicating officers are deemed judicial proceedings under sections 193 and 228 of the IPC, and the officer is deemed a civil court under the CrPC and CPC.

sections.title: Decision by Majority in Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: If the Members of a Bench consisting of two Members differ in opinion on any point, they shall state the point or points on which they differ, and make a reference to the Chairperson of the Appellate Tribunal who shall hear the point or points himself and such point or points shall be decided according to the opinion of the majority of the Members who have heard the case, including those who first heard it.
sections.plain_english_summary: Where two members of a bench disagree, the disputed point is referred to the Chairperson and decided by the majority of all members who have heard the case.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 52D of the Information Technology Act 2000. Where members of a two-member bench of the Appellate Tribunal differ in opinion, the disputed point is referred to the Chairperson who hears it, and the matter is decided by the majority opinion of all members who heard the case, including those who first heard it.

sections.title: Orders Constituting Appellate Tribunal - Finality
sections.clause_text: No order of the Central Government appointing any person as the Chairperson or the Member of a Appellate Tribunal shall be called in question in any manner and no act or proceeding before a Appellate Tribunal shall be called in question in any manner on the ground merely of any defect in the constitution of a Appellate Tribunal.
sections.plain_english_summary: Appointments to the Appellate Tribunal cannot be challenged, and acts or proceedings of the Tribunal cannot be invalidated merely due to a defect in its constitution.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 55 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Central Government orders appointing Chairpersons or Members of the Appellate Tribunal cannot be challenged in any manner. No act or proceeding of the Appellate Tribunal can be called into question merely on the ground of any defect in the constitution of the Tribunal.

sections.title: Appeal - Powers of Appellate Tribunal on Hearing
sections.clause_text: On receipt of an appeal under sub-section (1), the Appellate Tribunal may, after giving the parties to the appeal, an opportunity of being heard, pass such orders thereon as it thinks fit, confirming, modifying or setting aside the order appealed against.
sections.plain_english_summary: On receiving an appeal, the Appellate Tribunal may, after hearing the parties, confirm, modify, or set aside the order under appeal.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 57(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. After receiving an appeal and giving parties an opportunity to be heard, the Appellate Tribunal may confirm, modify or set aside the order appealed against.

sections.title: Appeal - Expeditious Disposal
sections.clause_text: The appeal filed before the Appellate Tribunal under sub-section (1) shall be dealt with by it as expeditiously as possible and endeavour shall be made by it to dispose of the appeal finally within six months from the date of receipt of the appeal.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Appellate Tribunal must deal with appeals as expeditiously as possible and endeavour to dispose of them within six months of receipt.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 57(6) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Appellate Tribunal must deal with appeals as expeditiously as possible and endeavour to dispose of each appeal within six months of receiving it.

sections.title: Appellate Tribunal - Procedure and Natural Justice
sections.clause_text: The Appellate Tribunal shall not be bound by the procedure laid down by the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 but shall be guided by the principles of natural justice and, subject to the other provisions of this Act and of any rules, the Appellate Tribunal shall have powers to regulate its own procedure including the place at which it shall have its sittings.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Appellate Tribunal is not bound by the CPC but must be guided by the principles of natural justice and may regulate its own procedure, including the place of sittings.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 58(1) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Appellate Tribunal is not bound by the Code of Civil Procedure 1908 but must be guided by the principles of natural justice. It may regulate its own procedure, including determining the place of its sittings, subject to the Act and applicable rules.

sections.title: Right to Legal Representation Before Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: The appellant may either appear in person or authorise one or more legal practitioners or any of its officers to present his or its case before the Appellate Tribunal.
sections.plain_english_summary: An appellant may appear in person or be represented by one or more legal practitioners or company officers before the Appellate Tribunal.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 59 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any appellant before the Appellate Tribunal has the right to appear in person or to be represented by one or more legal practitioners or by any of its officers.

sections.title: Limitation for Appeals to Appellate Tribunal
sections.clause_text: The provisions of the Limitation Act, 1963, shall, as far as may be, apply to an appeal made to the Appellate Tribunal.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Limitation Act, 1963 applies, as far as practicable, to appeals made to the Appellate Tribunal.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 60 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Limitation Act 1963 applies, as far as practicable, to appeals made to the Appellate Tribunal, supplementing the specific 45-day limitation period for appeals under section 57(3).

sections.title: Punishment for Sending Offensive Messages [Struck Down]
sections.clause_text: Any person who sends, by means of a computer resource or a communication device,— (a) any information that is grossly offensive or has menacing character; or (b) any information which he knows to be false, but for the purpose of causing annoyance, inconvenience, danger, obstruction, insult, injury, criminal intimidation, enmity, hatred or ill will, persistently by making use of such computer resource or a communication device; (c) any electronic mail or electronic mail message for the purpose of causing annoyance or inconvenience or to deceive or to mislead the addressee or recipient about the origin of such messages, shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years and with fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: Section 66A penalised sending grossly offensive, menacing, or false messages through computer resources or communication devices. This section was struck down by the Supreme Court of India in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) as unconstitutional.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 66A of the Information Technology Act 2000. This section criminalised sending grossly offensive or menacing information, knowingly false information causing annoyance or ill will, and deceptive electronic messages using computer resources or communication devices, with punishment of imprisonment up to three years and fine. The Supreme Court of India struck down section 66A in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India, AIR 2015 SC 1523, as unconstitutional for violating the right to freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.

sections.title: Power of Controller to Give Directions
sections.clause_text: The Controller may, by order, direct a Certifying Authority or any employee of such Authority to take such measures or cease carrying on such activities as specified in the order if those are necessary to ensure compliance with the provisions of this Act, rules or any regulations made thereunder. Any person who intentionally or knowingly fails to comply with any order under sub-section (1) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or a fine not exceeding one lakh rupees or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may direct a Certifying Authority or its employees to take action or stop activities to ensure compliance. Intentional non-compliance is an offence punishable with imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 68 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may by order direct a Certifying Authority or any of its employees to take specified measures or cease specified activities to ensure compliance with the Act and regulations. Intentional or knowing failure to comply with such an order is an offence punishable with imprisonment not exceeding two years or a fine not exceeding Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Interception and Monitoring - Safeguards
sections.clause_text: The procedure and safeguards subject to which such interception or monitoring or decryption may be carried out, shall be such as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: The procedure and safeguards for interception, monitoring, or decryption of information must be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The procedures and safeguards governing interception, monitoring, or decryption of information in computer resources under section 69(1) shall be as prescribed by the Central Government. These safeguards are intended to prevent misuse of surveillance powers.

sections.title: Failure to Assist Interception - Penalty
sections.clause_text: The subscriber or intermediary or any person who fails to assist the agency referred to in sub-section (3) shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any subscriber, intermediary, or person who fails to assist an authorised government agency with interception, monitoring, or decryption is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and a fine.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. A subscriber, intermediary, or any person who fails to assist a government agency authorised under section 69(1) to intercept, monitor, or decrypt information shall be punished with imprisonment for up to seven years and also liable to a fine.

sections.title: Blocking of Information - Safeguards
sections.clause_text: The procedure and safeguards subject to which such blocking for access by the public may be carried out, shall be such as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: The procedure and safeguards for blocking public access to information under section 69A must be prescribed by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69A(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The procedure and safeguards governing the blocking of information for public access under section 69A(1) shall be as prescribed by the Central Government.

sections.title: Blocking of Information - Penalty for Non-Compliance
sections.clause_text: The intermediary who fails to comply with the direction issued under sub-section (1) shall be punished with an imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years and also be liable to fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: An intermediary that fails to comply with a blocking direction under section 69A(1) is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and a fine.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69A(3) of the Information Technology Act 2000. An intermediary that fails to comply with a Central Government direction to block public access to information under section 69A(1) shall be punished with imprisonment for up to seven years and also liable to a fine.

sections.title: Traffic Data Monitoring - Penalty for Non-Compliance
sections.clause_text: Any intermediary who intentionally or knowingly contravenes the provisions of sub-section (2) shall be punished with an imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.plain_english_summary: An intermediary that intentionally or knowingly fails to provide technical assistance for traffic data monitoring under section 69B(2) is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and a fine.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 69B(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. An intermediary that intentionally or knowingly fails to provide technical assistance to an authorised government agency for monitoring and collecting traffic data under section 69B(2) shall be punished with imprisonment for up to three years and also be liable to a fine.

sections.title: Protected System - Authorisation of Access
sections.clause_text: The appropriate Government may, by order in writing, authorise the persons who are authorised to access protected systems notified under sub-section (1).
sections.plain_english_summary: The appropriate Government may by written order authorise specific persons to access protected computer systems.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70(2) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The appropriate Government may by written order specify and authorise the persons who are permitted to access protected systems declared under section 70(1). Any access to a protected system by an unauthorised person is an offence.

sections.title: Protected System - Information Security Practices
sections.clause_text: The Central Government shall prescribe the information security practices and procedures for such protected system.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government must prescribe information security practices and procedures specifically applicable to protected systems.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government is required to prescribe information security practices and procedures applicable to protected computer systems. These standards govern how operators of critical infrastructure must secure their systems.

sections.title: CERT-In Functions
sections.clause_text: The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team shall serve as the national agency for performing the following functions in the area of cyber security,— (a) collection, analysis and dissemination of information on cyber incidents; (b) forecast and alerts of cyber security incidents; (c) emergency measures for handling cyber security incidents; (d) coordination of cyber incidents response activities; (e) issue guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and white papers relating to information security practices, procedures, prevention, response and reporting of cyber incidents; (f) such other functions relating to cyber security as may be prescribed.
sections.plain_english_summary: CERT-In's statutory functions include collecting and analysing cyber incident data, issuing alerts and forecasts, handling cyber emergencies, coordinating incident response, and issuing information security guidelines and advisories.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70B(4) of the Information Technology Act 2000. CERT-In's national cyber security functions are: collection, analysis and dissemination of information on cyber incidents; forecast and alerts on cyber security incidents; emergency measures for handling incidents; coordination of response activities; and issuing guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and white papers on information security practices, prevention, response and reporting.

sections.title: CERT-In - Power to Call for Information and Give Directions
sections.clause_text: For carrying out the provisions of sub-section (4), the agency referred to in sub-section (1) may call for information and give direction to the service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate and any other person.
sections.plain_english_summary: CERT-In may call for information and give directions to service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates, and any other persons for carrying out its cyber security functions.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70B(6) of the Information Technology Act 2000. CERT-In may call for information and issue directions to service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and any other person for carrying out its cyber security functions under section 70B(4). Failure to comply is punishable with imprisonment up to one year or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: CERT-In - Penalty for Non-Compliance with Directions
sections.clause_text: Any service provider, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate or person who fails to provide the information called for or comply with the direction under sub-section (6), shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Failure to provide information or comply with CERT-In directions is punishable with imprisonment up to one year or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 70B(7) of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any service provider, intermediary, data centre, body corporate or person who fails to provide information called for or comply with directions given by CERT-In under section 70B(6) shall be punished with imprisonment for up to one year or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Penalty for Publishing False Electronic Signature Certificate
sections.clause_text: No person shall publish a electronic signature Certificate or otherwise make it available to any other person with the knowledge that— (a) the Certifying Authority listed in the certificate has not issued it; or (b) the subscriber listed in the certificate has not accepted it; or (c) the certificate has been revoked or suspended, unless such publication is for the purpose of verifying a electronic signature created prior to such suspension or revocation. Any person who contravenes the provisions of sub-section (1) shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Publishing or sharing an electronic signature certificate knowing it was not issued by the listed Certifying Authority, not accepted by the subscriber, or is revoked or suspended, is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 73 of the Information Technology Act 2000. It is an offence to publish or make available an electronic signature certificate knowing that the listed Certifying Authority did not issue it, the subscriber has not accepted it, or the certificate has been revoked or suspended, unless the purpose is to verify a signature created before suspension or revocation. The punishment is imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Publication of Electronic Signature Certificate for Fraudulent Purpose
sections.clause_text: Whoever knowingly creates, publishes or otherwise makes available a electronic signature Certificate for any fraudulent or unlawful purpose shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.plain_english_summary: Knowingly creating, publishing, or making available an electronic signature certificate for a fraudulent or unlawful purpose is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh, or both.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 74 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Whoever knowingly creates, publishes or makes available an electronic signature certificate for any fraudulent or unlawful purpose shall be punished with imprisonment for up to two years or a fine up to Rs 1 lakh or both.

sections.title: Confiscation
sections.clause_text: Any computer, computer system, floppies, compact disks, tape drives or any other accessories related thereto, in respect of which any provision of this Act, rules, orders or regulations made thereunder has been or is being contravened, shall be liable to confiscation: Provided that where it is established to the satisfaction of the court adjudicating the confiscation that the person in whose possession, power or control of any such computer, computer system, floppies, compact disks, tape drives or any other accessories relating thereto is found is not responsible for the contravention of the provisions of this Act, the court may, instead of making an order for confiscation, make such other order authorised by this Act against the person contravening.
sections.plain_english_summary: Computers, storage devices, and related accessories used in contravention of the Act are liable to confiscation, unless the possessor proves they were not responsible for the contravention.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 76 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Any computer, computer system, storage media, or accessories in respect of which a contravention of the Act has occurred or is occurring are liable to confiscation. A court may make an alternative order if the possessor is found not responsible for the contravention.

sections.title: Compensation, Penalties and Confiscation Not to Interfere with Other Punishment
sections.clause_text: No compensation awarded, penalty imposed or confiscation made under this Act shall prevent the award of compensation or imposition of any other penalty or punishment under any other law for the time being in force.
sections.plain_english_summary: Awards of compensation, penalties, or confiscations under this Act do not preclude other punishments or compensation under any other applicable law.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 77 of the Information Technology Act 2000. Compensation, penalties and confiscations under this Act are in addition to and do not bar compensation, penalties or punishment under any other law. This preserves the concurrent applicability of other penal laws.

sections.title: Application of the Act to Electronic Cheque and Truncated Cheque
sections.clause_text: The provisions of this Act, for the time being in force, shall apply to, or in relation to, electronic cheques and the truncated cheques subject to such modifications and amendments as may be necessary for carrying out the purposes of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 by the Central Government, in consultation with the Reserve Bank of India, by notification in the Official Gazette.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Act applies to electronic cheques and truncated cheques with such modifications as the Central Government may notify in consultation with the Reserve Bank of India.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 81A of the Information Technology Act 2000. The provisions of the IT Act apply to electronic cheques and truncated cheques with modifications and amendments the Central Government may prescribe by notification in consultation with the Reserve Bank of India, to carry out the purposes of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881.

sections.title: Controller and Deputy Controller as Public Servants
sections.clause_text: The Controller, the Deputy Controller and the Assistant Controllers shall be deemed to be public servants within the meaning of section 21 of the Indian Penal Code.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller, Deputy Controller, and Assistant Controllers are deemed public servants under section 21 of the Indian Penal Code.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 82 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller, Deputy Controller and Assistant Controllers are deemed to be public servants within the meaning of section 21 of the Indian Penal Code 1860, making them subject to penal provisions applicable to public servants.

sections.title: Power of Central Government to Give Directions to State Governments
sections.clause_text: The Central Government may give directions to any State Government as to the carrying into execution in the State of any of the provisions of this Act or of any rule, regulation or order made thereunder.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may issue directions to State Governments on implementing the provisions of the Act and rules made thereunder.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 83 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may give directions to any State Government regarding the execution within the State of any provisions of the IT Act or rules, regulations, or orders made thereunder.

sections.title: Removal of Difficulties
sections.clause_text: If any difficulty arises in giving effect to the provisions of this Act, the Central Government may, by order published in the Official Gazette, make such provisions not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act as appear to it to be necessary or expedient for removing the difficulty: Provided that no order shall be made under this section after the expiry of a period of two years from the commencement of this Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Central Government may make provisions to remove difficulties in implementing the Act by order published in the Official Gazette, but only within two years of the Act's commencement.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 86 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Central Government may by order in the Official Gazette make provisions consistent with the Act to remove any difficulties arising in its implementation. Such orders may not be made after two years from commencement of the Act.

sections.title: Power of Controller to Make Regulations
sections.clause_text: The Controller may, after consultation with the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee and with the previous approval of the Central Government, by notification in the Official Gazette, make regulations consistent with this Act and the rules made thereunder to carry out the purposes of this Act. Such regulations may provide for: maintenance of data base records of Certifying Authorities; conditions for recognition of foreign Certifying Authorities; terms and conditions for licence; standards to be observed by Certifying Authorities; manner of disclosure by Certifying Authorities; particulars for electronic signature certificate applications; and manner in which subscribers shall communicate private key compromise.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Controller may make regulations covering Certifying Authority databases, foreign authority recognition, licensing conditions, disclosure standards, certificate application particulars, and private key compromise notification, after consulting the Advisory Committee and with Central Government approval.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 89 of the Information Technology Act 2000. The Controller may make regulations, after consulting the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee and with Central Government approval, covering: disclosure records of Certifying Authorities; conditions for recognising foreign Certifying Authorities; licence terms and conditions; standards for Certifying Authorities; manner of disclosure; particulars required for electronic signature certificate applications; and manner of notifying private key compromise.

sections.title: Power of State Government to Make Rules
sections.clause_text: The State Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules to carry out the provisions of this Act. In particular, and without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing power, such rules may provide for all or any of the following matters, namely:— (a) the electronic form in which filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment shall be effected under sub-section (1) of section 6; (b) for matters specified in sub-section (2) of section 6.
sections.plain_english_summary: State Governments may make rules to carry out the provisions of the Act, particularly for prescribing electronic forms for filings, grant of licences, and payments under section 6.
sections.retrieval_text: Section 90 of the Information Technology Act 2000. State Governments may make rules by notification in the Official Gazette for carrying out the Act's provisions, particularly prescribing the electronic form for Government filings, issue, grant, receipt or payment under section 6(1) and related matters under section 6(2).

sections.title: Electronic Signature or Electronic Authentication Techniques and Procedures
sections.clause_text: The Second Schedule specifies the electronic signature or electronic authentication technique and procedure for the purposes of section 3A. The Central Government may by notification add to or omit any electronic signature or electronic authentication technique from the Second Schedule, provided that no technique shall be specified unless it is reliable.
sections.plain_english_summary: The Second Schedule lists the approved electronic signature and authentication techniques for the purposes of section 3A. The Central Government may add or remove techniques from the Schedule by notification, subject to a reliability requirement.
sections.retrieval_text: Second Schedule to the Information Technology Act 2000. The Second Schedule specifies approved electronic signature and electronic authentication techniques and procedures under section 3A. The Central Government may by notification amend the Schedule by adding or omitting techniques, but only reliable techniques may be included.

sections.title: Short Title
sections.clause_text: These rules may be called the Information Technology (Reasonable security practices and procedures and sensitive personal data or information) Rules, 2011.
sections.plain_english_summary: These rules are officially titled the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 1(1) of the SPDI Rules 2011. These rules are officially titled the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011. They are made under clause (ob) of sub-section (2) of section 87 read with section 43A of the Information Technology Act 2000.

sections.title: Commencement
sections.clause_text: They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.
sections.plain_english_summary: The SPDI Rules 2011 came into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette, i.e. 11th April 2011.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 1(2) of the SPDI Rules 2011. The rules came into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette, which was 11th April 2011.

sections.title: Definition - Act
sections.clause_text: "Act" means the Information Technology Act, 2000 (21 of 2000).
sections.plain_english_summary: The term 'Act' in the SPDI Rules refers to the Information Technology Act, 2000.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(a) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines Act as the Information Technology Act 2000. All references to the Act in these rules refer to the IT Act 2000.

sections.title: Definition - Biometrics
sections.clause_text: "Biometrics" means the technologies that measure and analyse human body characteristics, such as 'fingerprints', 'eye retinas and irises', 'voice patterns', 'facial patterns', 'hand measurements' and 'DNA' for authentication purposes.
sections.plain_english_summary: Biometrics refers to technologies that measure and analyse human body characteristics such as fingerprints, iris scans, voice patterns, facial patterns, hand measurements, and DNA for authentication purposes.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(b) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines biometrics as technologies that measure and analyse human body characteristics including fingerprints, eye retinas and irises, voice patterns, facial patterns, hand measurements and DNA for authentication purposes. Biometric information is classified as sensitive personal data or information under Rule 3.

sections.title: Definition - Body Corporate
sections.clause_text: "Body corporate" means the body corporate as defined in clause (i) of explanation to section 43A of the Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: Body corporate has the same meaning as in the explanation to section 43A of the IT Act 2000, which includes any company, firm, sole proprietorship or other association of individuals engaged in commercial or professional activities.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(c) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines body corporate by reference to clause (i) of the explanation to section 43A of the IT Act 2000, which covers any company, firm, sole proprietorship or other association of individuals engaged in commercial or professional activities. Body corporates are the primary duty-bearers under these Rules.

sections.title: Definition - Cyber Incidents
sections.clause_text: "Cyber incidents" means any real or suspected adverse event in relation to cyber security that violates an explicitly or implicitly applicable security policy resulting in unauthorised access, denial of service or disruption, unauthorised use of a computer resource for processing or storage of information or changes to data, information without authorisation.
sections.plain_english_summary: A cyber incident is any real or suspected adverse event that violates a security policy, resulting in unauthorised access, denial of service, disruption, unauthorised use of computer resources, or unauthorised data changes.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(d) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines cyber incidents as any real or suspected adverse event relating to cyber security that violates an explicitly or implicitly applicable security policy, resulting in unauthorised access, denial of service or disruption, unauthorised use of a computer resource, or unauthorised changes to data or information.

sections.title: Definition - Password
sections.clause_text: "Password" means a secret word or phrase or code or passphrase or secret key, or encryption or decryption keys that one uses to gain admittance or access to information.
sections.plain_english_summary: A password is any secret word, phrase, code, passphrase, secret key, or encryption or decryption key used to gain access to information.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(h) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines password as a secret word, phrase, code, passphrase, secret key, or encryption or decryption keys used to gain access to information. Passwords are classified as sensitive personal data or information under Rule 3.

sections.title: Definition - Personal Information
sections.clause_text: "Personal information" means any information that relates to a natural person, which, either directly or indirectly, in combination with other information available or likely to be available with a body corporate, is capable of identifying such person.
sections.plain_english_summary: Personal information is any information relating to a natural person that can directly or indirectly identify that person, including when combined with other information available to a body corporate.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(1)(i) of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines personal information as any information relating to a natural person that, either directly or indirectly, or in combination with other information available or likely to be available with a body corporate, is capable of identifying that person. This definition covers both directly identifying information and information that becomes identifying when combined with other data.

sections.title: Interpretation - Words Defined in the Act
sections.clause_text: All other words and expressions used and not defined in these rules but defined in the Act shall have the meanings respectively assigned to them in the Act.
sections.plain_english_summary: Any term used in the SPDI Rules that is not defined in these Rules but is defined in the IT Act 2000 shall carry the meaning assigned in the IT Act.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 2(2) of the SPDI Rules 2011. All words and expressions used in these Rules that are not defined herein but are defined in the Information Technology Act 2000 shall have the meanings assigned to them in the Act.

sections.title: Sensitive Personal Data or Information - Definition
sections.clause_text: Sensitive personal data or information of a person means such personal information which consists of information relating to;— (i) password; (ii) financial information such as Bank account or credit card or debit card or other payment instrument details; (iii) physical, physiological and mental health condition; (iv) sexual orientation; (v) medical records and history; (vi) Biometric information; (vii) any detail relating to the above clauses as provided to body corporate for providing service; and (viii) any of the information received under above clauses by body corporate for processing, stored or processed under lawful contract or otherwise: provided that, any information that is freely available or accessible in public domain or furnished under the Right to Information Act, 2005 or any other law for the time being in force shall not be regarded as sensitive personal data or information for the purposes of these rules.
sections.plain_english_summary: Sensitive personal data or information includes passwords, financial details, health conditions, sexual orientation, medical records, biometric information, and any related details provided to a body corporate. Publicly available information or information disclosed under the RTI Act is excluded.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 3 of the SPDI Rules 2011 defines sensitive personal data or information as personal information consisting of passwords, financial information such as bank account, credit card or debit card details, physical, physiological and mental health conditions, sexual orientation, medical records and history, biometric information, and any related details provided to a body corporate for providing services or processing under a lawful contract. Information freely available in the public domain or furnished under the Right to Information Act 2005 is expressly excluded from this definition.

sections.title: Privacy Policy - Obligation to Publish
sections.clause_text: The body corporate or any person who on behalf of body corporate collects, receives, possess, stores, deals or handle information of provider of information, shall provide a privacy policy for handling of or dealing in personal information including sensitive personal data or information and ensure that the same are available for view by such providers of information who has provided such information under lawful contract. Such policy shall be published on website of body corporate or any person on its behalf and shall provide for— (i) Clear and easily accessible statements of its practices and policies; (ii) type of personal or sensitive personal data or information collected under rule 3; (iii) purpose of collection and usage of such information; (iv) disclosure of information including sensitive personal data or information as provided in rule 6; (v) reasonable security practices and procedures as provided under rule 8.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate that collects, stores, or handles personal information must publish a privacy policy on its website. The policy must clearly state its practices, types of data collected, purpose of collection, disclosure practices, and security measures.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 4(1) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires every body corporate or person acting on its behalf that collects, receives, possesses, stores, deals with or handles personal information to publish a privacy policy on its website. The policy must be available for viewing by information providers and must include clear statements of practices and policies, the type of personal or sensitive personal data collected under Rule 3, the purpose of collection and usage, disclosure practices under Rule 6, and reasonable security practices under Rule 8.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Written Consent Requirement
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf shall obtain consent in writing through letter or Fax or email from the provider of the sensitive personal data or information regarding purpose of usage before collection of such information.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate must obtain written consent — by letter, fax, or email — from the person providing sensitive personal data or information, regarding the purpose of use, before collecting it.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(1) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires a body corporate or any person acting on its behalf to obtain written consent from the provider of sensitive personal data or information before collecting such information. The consent must specify the purpose of usage and may be obtained through letter, fax or email.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Lawful Purpose and Necessity
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf shall not collect sensitive personal data or information unless — (a) the information is collected for a lawful purpose connected with a function or activity of the body corporate or any person on its behalf; and (b) the collection of the sensitive personal data or information is considered necessary for that purpose.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate may only collect sensitive personal data or information if the collection is for a lawful purpose connected with its functions or activities and is considered necessary for that purpose.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(2) of the SPDI Rules 2011 prohibits a body corporate or any person acting on its behalf from collecting sensitive personal data or information unless two conditions are met: the information is collected for a lawful purpose connected with the body corporate's functions or activities, and the collection is necessary for that purpose. This establishes a necessity and lawful purpose test for collection.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Notice Requirements
sections.clause_text: While collecting information directly from the person concerned, the body corporate or any person on its behalf shall take such steps as are, in the circumstances, reasonable to ensure that the person concerned is having the knowledge of — (a) the fact that the information is being collected; (b) the purpose for which the information is being collected; (c) the intended recipients of the information; and (d) the name and address of — (i) the agency that is collecting the information; and (ii) the agency that will retain the information.
sections.plain_english_summary: When collecting information directly from a person, the body corporate must take reasonable steps to ensure the person knows that their data is being collected, the purpose, the intended recipients, and the identity and address of both the collecting and retaining agencies.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(3) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires a body corporate or person acting on its behalf, when collecting information directly from the person concerned, to take reasonable steps to ensure that the person knows: that the information is being collected; the purpose of collection; the intended recipients; and the name and address of the agency collecting the information and the agency that will retain it.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Retention Limitation
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf holding sensitive personal data or information shall not retain that information for longer than is required for the purposes for which the information may lawfully be used or is otherwise required under any other law for the time being in force.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate must not retain sensitive personal data or information for longer than is required for the lawful purposes for which it was collected, unless another law requires retention.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(4) of the SPDI Rules 2011 imposes a data retention limitation on body corporates. Sensitive personal data or information must not be retained longer than is required for the purposes for which it may lawfully be used or as required under any other applicable law. This establishes a purpose-linked retention obligation.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Purpose Limitation
sections.clause_text: The information collected shall be used for the purpose for which it has been collected.
sections.plain_english_summary: Information collected by a body corporate must only be used for the purpose for which it was collected.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(5) of the SPDI Rules 2011 establishes a purpose limitation principle: information collected by a body corporate must be used only for the specific purpose for which it was collected and not for any other purpose.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Right to Review and Correct
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf permit the providers of information, as and when requested by them, to review the information they had provided and ensure that any personal information or sensitive personal data or information found to be inaccurate or deficient shall be corrected or amended as feasible: Provided that a body corporate shall not be responsible for the authenticity of the personal information or sensitive personal data or information supplied by the provider of information to such body corporate or any other person acting on behalf of such body corporate.
sections.plain_english_summary: Information providers have the right to review their data held by a body corporate and have inaccurate or deficient information corrected or amended. However, the body corporate is not responsible for the authenticity of information originally supplied by the provider.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(6) of the SPDI Rules 2011 grants information providers the right to review their personal information or sensitive personal data held by a body corporate upon request, and requires the body corporate to correct or amend any inaccurate or deficient information as feasible. However, the body corporate is not responsible for the authenticity of information originally supplied by the provider.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Opt-Out and Withdrawal of Consent
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf shall, prior to the collection of information including sensitive personal data or information, provide an option to the provider of the information to not to provide the data or information sought to be collected. The provider of information shall, at any time while availing the services or otherwise, also have an option to withdraw its consent given earlier to the body corporate. Such withdrawal of the consent shall be sent in writing to the body corporate. In the case of provider of information not providing or later on withdrawing his consent, the body corporate shall have the option not to provide goods or services for which the said information was sought.
sections.plain_english_summary: Before collecting sensitive personal data, the body corporate must offer an opt-out option. Individuals may also withdraw previously given consent at any time in writing. If consent is not given or is withdrawn, the body corporate may choose not to provide the goods or services that required that information.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(7) of the SPDI Rules 2011 establishes two rights for information providers: the right to opt out before any collection of sensitive personal data or information, and the right to withdraw previously given consent at any time in writing. The body corporate must provide an opt-out option prior to collection. Upon withdrawal of consent, the body corporate has the option to withhold goods or services for which the information was sought.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Security Obligation
sections.clause_text: Body corporate or any person on its behalf shall keep the information secure as provided in rule 8.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate must keep all collected information secure in accordance with the reasonable security practices and procedures set out in Rule 8.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(8) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires a body corporate or any person acting on its behalf to keep all collected information secure in accordance with the reasonable security practices and procedures prescribed under Rule 8.

sections.title: Collection of Information - Grievance Officer
sections.clause_text: Body corporate shall address any discrepancies and grievances of their provider of the information with respect to processing of information in a time bound manner. For this purpose, the body corporate shall designate a Grievance Officer and publish his name and contact details on its website. The Grievance Officer shall redress the grievances of provider of information expeditiously but within one month from the date of receipt of grievance.
sections.plain_english_summary: Every body corporate must designate a Grievance Officer and publish their name and contact details on its website. The Grievance Officer must resolve grievances relating to processing of information within one month of receipt.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 5(9) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires every body corporate to designate a Grievance Officer and publish their name and contact details on its website to address discrepancies and grievances relating to the processing of information. The Grievance Officer must redress grievances expeditiously and within one month from the date of receipt.

sections.title: Disclosure of Information - Prior Consent Requirement
sections.clause_text: Disclosure of sensitive personal data or information by body corporate to any third party shall require prior permission from the provider of such information, who has provided such information under lawful contract or otherwise, unless such disclosure has been agreed to in the contract between the body corporate and provider of information, or where the disclosure is necessary for compliance of a legal obligation: Provided that the information shall be shared, without obtaining prior consent from provider of information, with Government agencies mandated under the law to obtain information including sensitive personal data or information for the purpose of verification of identity, or for prevention, detection, investigation including cyber incidents, prosecution, and punishment of offences. The Government agency shall send a request in writing to the body corporate possessing the sensitive personal data or information stating clearly the purpose of seeking such information. The Government agency shall also state that the information so obtained shall not be published or shared with any other person.
sections.plain_english_summary: Disclosing sensitive personal data to a third party requires prior consent from the information provider, unless agreed in contract or required by legal obligation. Government agencies may obtain such data without prior consent for identity verification, investigation, or prosecution, but must submit a written request stating the purpose and undertaking not to publish or further share the information.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 6(1) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires a body corporate to obtain prior permission from the information provider before disclosing sensitive personal data or information to any third party, unless the disclosure was agreed in the original contract or is necessary for a legal obligation. An exception exists for Government agencies mandated by law to obtain sensitive personal data for identity verification, prevention, detection, investigation of cyber incidents, prosecution and punishment of offences. Such agencies must send a written request stating the purpose and confirming the information will not be published or shared further.

sections.title: Disclosure of Information - Disclosure by Court Order
sections.clause_text: Notwithstanding anything contain in sub-rule (1), any sensitive personal data or information shall be disclosed to any third party by an order under the law for the time being in force.
sections.plain_english_summary: Sensitive personal data or information may be disclosed to a third party pursuant to an order made under any law in force, overriding the consent requirement in sub-rule (1).
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 6(2) of the SPDI Rules 2011 provides that notwithstanding the prior consent requirement in Rule 6(1), sensitive personal data or information must be disclosed to any third party pursuant to an order made under any applicable law. This covers disclosures required by court orders or other legal orders.

sections.title: Disclosure of Information - Prohibition on Publication
sections.clause_text: The body corporate or any person on its behalf shall not publish the sensitive personal data or information.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate or anyone acting on its behalf is prohibited from publishing sensitive personal data or information.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 6(3) of the SPDI Rules 2011 imposes an absolute prohibition on a body corporate or any person acting on its behalf from publishing sensitive personal data or information.

sections.title: Disclosure of Information - Onward Disclosure Prohibition
sections.clause_text: The third party receiving the sensitive personal data or information from body corporate or any person on its behalf under sub-rule (1) shall not disclose it further.
sections.plain_english_summary: A third party that receives sensitive personal data from a body corporate is prohibited from disclosing it to any other party.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 6(4) of the SPDI Rules 2011 prohibits any third party that receives sensitive personal data or information from a body corporate under Rule 6(1) from further disclosing that information to any other person.

sections.title: Transfer of Information - Cross-Border Transfer Conditions
sections.clause_text: A body corporate or any person on its behalf may transfer sensitive personal data or information including any information, to any other body corporate or a person in India, or located in any other country, that ensures the same level of data protection that is adhered to by the body corporate as provided for under these Rules. The transfer may be allowed only if it is necessary for the performance of the lawful contract between the body corporate or any person on its behalf and provider of information or where such person has consented to data transfer.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate may transfer sensitive personal data to another body corporate or person in India or abroad only if the recipient ensures an equivalent level of data protection, and the transfer is necessary for performance of a lawful contract or the information provider has consented to the transfer.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 7 of the SPDI Rules 2011 permits a body corporate or person acting on its behalf to transfer sensitive personal data or information to another body corporate or person in India or abroad, subject to two conditions: the recipient must ensure the same level of data protection as required under these Rules, and the transfer must be necessary for performance of a lawful contract with the information provider or the information provider must have consented to the transfer.

sections.title: Reasonable Security Practices - Compliance Standard
sections.clause_text: A body corporate or a person on its behalf shall be considered to have complied with reasonable security practices and procedures, if they have implemented such security practices and standards and have a comprehensive documented information security programme and information security policies that contain managerial, technical, operational and physical security control measures that are commensurate with the information assets being protected with the nature of business. In the event of an information security breach, the body corporate or a person on its behalf shall be required to demonstrate, as and when called upon to do so by the agency mandated under the law, that they have implemented security control measures as per their documented information security programme and information security policies.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate complies with reasonable security practices by implementing a comprehensive documented information security programme with managerial, technical, operational and physical controls appropriate to the information assets and nature of business. In the event of a breach, the body corporate must be able to demonstrate implementation of these controls to the mandated agency.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 8(1) of the SPDI Rules 2011 sets out the standard for compliance with reasonable security practices and procedures. A body corporate is considered compliant if it has implemented a comprehensive documented information security programme and policies containing managerial, technical, operational and physical security controls commensurate with the nature of its business and the information assets being protected. In the event of an information security breach, the body corporate must demonstrate to the mandated agency that it implemented the documented security controls.

sections.title: Reasonable Security Practices - ISO/IEC 27001 Standard
sections.clause_text: The international Standard IS/ISO/IEC 27001 on "Information Technology - Security Techniques - Information Security Management System - Requirements" is one such standard referred to in sub-rule (1).
sections.plain_english_summary: The international standard IS/ISO/IEC 27001 on Information Security Management Systems is recognised as one of the standards satisfying the reasonable security practices requirement under Rule 8(1).
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 8(2) of the SPDI Rules 2011 recognises the international standard IS/ISO/IEC 27001 on Information Technology Security Techniques — Information Security Management System Requirements as a standard that satisfies the reasonable security practices and procedures requirement under Rule 8(1).

sections.title: Reasonable Security Practices - Industry Self-Regulation Approval
sections.clause_text: Any industry association or an entity formed by such an association, whose members are self-regulating by following other than IS/ISO/IEC codes of best practices for data protection as per sub-rule(1), shall get its codes of best practices duly approved and notified by the Central Government for effective implementation.
sections.plain_english_summary: Industry associations or entities using self-regulatory codes of best practices for data protection that are different from ISO/IEC standards must get their codes approved and notified by the Central Government.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 8(3) of the SPDI Rules 2011 requires any industry association or entity whose members follow codes of best practices for data protection other than IS/ISO/IEC standards to obtain approval and notification from the Central Government for those codes to be recognised as satisfying the reasonable security practices requirement under Rule 8(1).

sections.title: Reasonable Security Practices - Audit Requirement
sections.clause_text: The body corporate or a person on its behalf who have implemented either IS/ISO/IEC 27001 standard or the codes of best practices for data protection as approved and notified under sub-rule (3) shall be deemed to have complied with reasonable security practices and procedures provided that such standard or the codes of best practices have been certified or audited on a regular basis by entities through independent auditor, duly approved by the Central Government. The audit of reasonable security practices and procedures shall be carried out by an auditor at least once a year or as and when the body corporate or a person on its behalf undertake significant upgradation of its process and computer resource.
sections.plain_english_summary: A body corporate that implements ISO/IEC 27001 or approved industry codes of best practices is deemed compliant with reasonable security practices, provided the standard or code is regularly certified or audited by an independent auditor approved by the Central Government. The audit must be conducted at least once a year or whenever a significant upgrade of processes or computer resources takes place.
sections.retrieval_text: Rule 8(4) of the SPDI Rules 2011 provides that a body corporate implementing IS/ISO/IEC 27001 or Central Government-approved industry codes of best practices is deemed compliant with reasonable security practices and procedures, provided the implementation is certified or audited on a regular basis by an independent auditor approved by the Central Government. Audits must be conducted at least annually or whenever significant upgrades to processes or computer resources are undertaken.

sections.title: CERT-In Directions 2022 - Legal Basis and Authority
sections.clause_text: Directions under sub-section (6) of section 70B of the Information Technology Act, 2000 relating to information security practices, procedure, prevention, response and reporting of cyber incidents for Safe & Trusted Internet. Issued on 28 April 2022. These directions are issued in the interest of the sovereignty or integrity of India, defence of India, security of the state, friendly relations with foreign states or public order or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence using computer resource or for handling of any cyber incident, to augment and strengthen cyber security in the country.
sections.plain_english_summary: These directions are issued by CERT-In on 28 April 2022 under section 70B(6) of the IT Act 2000, to strengthen cyber security in India. They are binding on service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates, and government organisations and became effective 60 days from the date of issue.
sections.retrieval_text: The CERT-In Directions issued on 28 April 2022 under sub-section (6) of section 70B of the Information Technology Act 2000 are binding directions relating to information security practices, procedure, prevention, response and reporting of cyber incidents for safe and trusted internet. CERT-In is empowered under section 70B(6) to call for information and give directions to service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and government organisations. These directions came into effect 60 days from 28 April 2022 and non-compliance may invite punitive action under section 70B(7) of the IT Act 2000.

sections.title: NTP Server Synchronisation
sections.clause_text: All service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate and Government organisations shall connect to the Network Time Protocol (NTP) Server of National Informatics Centre (NIC) or National Physical Laboratory (NPL) or with NTP servers traceable to these NTP servers, for synchronisation of all their ICT systems clocks. Entities having ICT infrastructure spanning multiple geographies may also use accurate and standard time source other than NPL and NIC, however it is to be ensured that their time source shall not deviate from NPL and NIC.
sections.plain_english_summary: All covered entities must synchronise all their ICT system clocks with the NTP servers of NIC or NPL, or NTP servers traceable to these. Entities with multi-geography infrastructure may use other accurate time sources provided they do not deviate from NIC and NPL.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (i) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 requires all service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and government organisations to synchronise all ICT system clocks with the NTP Server of the National Informatics Centre (NIC) or National Physical Laboratory (NPL), or NTP servers traceable to these. Entities with ICT infrastructure spanning multiple geographies may use other accurate and standard time sources, but such sources must not deviate from NIC and NPL. This obligation ensures accurate and consistent time-stamping across all systems for cyber incident investigation.

sections.title: Mandatory Cyber Incident Reporting Within 6 Hours
sections.clause_text: Any service provider, intermediary, data centre, body corporate and Government organisation shall mandatorily report cyber incidents as mentioned in Annexure I to CERT-In within 6 hours of noticing such incidents or being brought to notice about such incidents. The incidents can be reported to CERT-In via email (incident@cert-in.org.in), Phone (1800-11-4949) and Fax (1800-11-6969). The details regarding methods and formats of reporting cyber security incidents is also published on the website of CERT-In www.cert-in.org.in and will be updated from time to time.
sections.plain_english_summary: All covered entities must mandatorily report cyber incidents listed in Annexure I to CERT-In within 6 hours of noticing or being informed of such incidents. Reports may be submitted via email, phone, or fax.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (ii) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 requires all service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and government organisations to mandatorily report cyber incidents specified in Annexure I to CERT-In within 6 hours of noticing such incidents or being brought to notice of them. Incidents can be reported via email at incident@cert-in.org.in, by phone at 1800-11-4949, or by fax at 1800-11-6969. Reporting formats are published on the CERT-In website and updated from time to time.

sections.title: Compliance with CERT-In Orders and Point of Contact Designation
sections.clause_text: When required by order/direction of CERT-In, for the purposes of cyber incident response, protective and preventive actions related to cyber incidents, the service provider/intermediary/data centre/body corporate is mandated to take action or provide information or any such assistance to CERT-In, which may contribute towards cyber security mitigation actions and enhanced cyber security situational awareness. The order/direction may include the format of the information that is required (up to and including near real-time), and a specified timeframe in which it is required, which should be adhered to and compliance provided to CERT-In, else it would be treated as non-compliance of this direction. The service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate and Government organisations shall designate a Point of Contact to interface with CERT-In. The Information relating to a Point of Contact shall be sent to CERT-In in the format specified at Annexure II and shall be updated from time to time. All communications from CERT-In seeking information and providing directions for compliance shall be sent to the said Point of Contact.
sections.plain_english_summary: Covered entities must comply with CERT-In orders and directions for cyber incident response, including providing information in specified formats and timeframes. Each entity must designate a Point of Contact (PoC) to interface with CERT-In and submit PoC details in the Annexure II format. All CERT-In communications will be sent to the designated PoC.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (iii) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 mandates that service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and government organisations comply with CERT-In orders and directions for cyber incident response and preventive actions, including providing information or assistance in specified formats and timeframes. Failure to comply within the specified timeframe constitutes non-compliance. Each covered entity must designate a Point of Contact to interface with CERT-In and submit their details in the format specified in Annexure II, keeping it updated. All CERT-In communications will be directed to the designated Point of Contact.

sections.title: ICT Log Retention for 180 Days Within Indian Jurisdiction
sections.clause_text: All service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate and Government organisations shall mandatorily enable logs of all their ICT systems and maintain them securely for a rolling period of 180 days and the same shall be maintained within the Indian jurisdiction. These should be provided to CERT-In along with reporting of any incident or when ordered/directed by CERT-In.
sections.plain_english_summary: All covered entities must enable and securely maintain logs of all ICT systems for a rolling period of 180 days. Logs must be stored within India and must be provided to CERT-In when reporting an incident or when directed by CERT-In.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (iv) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 requires all service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates and government organisations to mandatorily enable and securely maintain logs of all ICT systems for a rolling period of 180 days. The logs must be maintained within Indian jurisdiction and must be provided to CERT-In when reporting any incident or when ordered or directed by CERT-In to do so.

sections.title: Data Centre, VPS, Cloud and VPN Provider - Subscriber Registration and Record Retention
sections.clause_text: Data Centres, Virtual Private Server (VPS) providers, Cloud Service providers and Virtual Private Network Service (VPN Service) providers, shall be required to register the following accurate information which must be maintained by them for a period of 5 years or longer duration as mandated by the law after any cancellation or withdrawal of the registration as the case may be: a. Validated names of subscribers/customers hiring the services; b. Period of hire including dates; c. IPs allotted to / being used by the members; d. Email address and IP address and time stamp used at the time of registration / on-boarding; e. Purpose for hiring services; f. Validated address and contact numbers; g. Ownership pattern of the subscribers / customers hiring services.
sections.plain_english_summary: Data centres, VPS providers, cloud service providers, and VPN service providers must register and maintain accurate subscriber information — including names, hire period, IP addresses, email addresses, registration timestamps, purpose, address, contact numbers, and ownership pattern — for at least 5 years after cancellation or withdrawal of registration.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (v) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 requires data centres, Virtual Private Server providers, cloud service providers and VPN service providers to register and maintain accurate subscriber and customer information. The information to be maintained includes validated names of subscribers, period of hire, IPs allotted or used, email address and IP address with timestamp at registration or on-boarding, purpose for hiring services, validated address and contact numbers, and ownership pattern of subscribers. This information must be maintained for a period of five years or longer as mandated by law, calculated from the date of any cancellation or withdrawal of registration.

sections.title: Virtual Asset Service Providers - KYC and Transaction Record Retention
sections.clause_text: The virtual asset service providers, virtual asset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers (as defined by Ministry of Finance from time to time) shall mandatorily maintain all information obtained as part of Know Your Customer (KYC) and records of financial transactions for a period of five years so as to ensure cyber security in the area of payments and financial markets for citizens while protecting their data, fundamental rights and economic freedom in view of the growth of virtual assets. For the purpose of KYC, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Directions 2016 / Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) circular dated April 24, 2020 / Department of Telecom (DoT) notice September 21, 2021 mandated procedures as amended from time to time may be referred to as per Annexure III. With respect to transaction records, accurate information shall be maintained in such a way that individual transaction can be reconstructed along with the relevant elements comprising of, but not limited to, information relating to the identification of the relevant parties including IP addresses along with timestamps and time zones, transaction ID, the public keys (or equivalent identifiers), addresses or accounts involved (or equivalent identifiers), the nature and date of the transaction, and the amount transferred.
sections.plain_english_summary: Virtual asset service providers, virtual asset exchange providers, and custodian wallet providers must maintain KYC information and financial transaction records for five years. Transaction records must be detailed enough to reconstruct individual transactions, including party identification, IP addresses with timestamps, transaction ID, public keys, accounts involved, nature and date of transaction, and amount transferred.
sections.retrieval_text: Direction (vi) of the CERT-In Directions 2022 requires virtual asset service providers, virtual asset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers, as defined by the Ministry of Finance, to mandatorily maintain all KYC information and records of financial transactions for a period of five years. KYC procedures must follow RBI Directions 2016, SEBI circular dated April 24 2020, or DoT notice dated September 21 2021 as per Annexure III. Transaction records must be maintained in a way that allows individual transactions to be reconstructed, including identification of relevant parties, IP addresses with timestamps and time zones, transaction IDs, public keys or equivalent identifiers, addresses or accounts involved, the nature and date of the transaction, and the amount transferred.

sections.title: Types of Cyber Security Incidents Mandatorily Reportable to CERT-In
sections.clause_text: Types of cyber security incidents mandatorily to be reported by service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporate and Government organisations to CERT-In: i. Targeted scanning/probing of critical networks/systems; ii. Compromise of critical systems/information; iii. Unauthorised access of IT systems/data; iv. Defacement of website or intrusion into a website and unauthorised changes such as inserting malicious code, links to external websites etc.; v. Malicious code attacks such as spreading of virus/worm/Trojan/Bots/Spyware/Ransomware/Cryptominers; vi. Attack on servers such as Database, Mail and DNS and network devices such as Routers; vii. sections.plain_english_summary: Annexure I lists 20 categories of cyber security incidents that must be mandatorily reported to CERT-In within 6 hours. sections.title: KYC Requirements - Officially Valid Documents and Business Entity Due Diligence
sections.clause_text: For the purpose of KYC, any of following Officially Valid Document (OVD) as a measure of identification procedure prescribed by the Reserve Bank of India (Know Your Customer (KYC)) Directions, 2016 / Securities and Exchange Board of India Clarification on Know Your Client (KYC) Process and Use of Technology for KYC vide Circular SEBI/HO/MIRSD/DOP/CIR/P/2020/73 dated April 24, 2020 / The Department of Telecom File No: 800-12/2021-AS.II dated September 21, 2021 on Self-KYC (S-KYC) as an alternate process for issuing of new mobile connections to Local and Outstation category customers, shall be used and maintained: a. The passport; b. The driving license; c. Proof of possession of Aadhaar number; d. The Voter's Identity Card issued by the Election Commission of India; e. Job card issued by NREGA duly signed by an officer of the State Government; f. Letter issued by the National Population Register containing details of name and address; g. Validated phone number; h. Trading account number and details, Bank account number and bank details. For the purpose of KYC for business entities (B2B), documents mentioned in the Customer Due Diligence (CDD) process prescribed in Reserve Bank of India Master Direction - Know Your Customer (KYC) Direction, 2016 as updated from time to time shall be used and maintained.
sections.plain_english_summary: Annexure III specifies the Officially Valid Documents (OVDs) required for KYC under Direction (vi). For individual customers these include passport, driving licence, Aadhaar, voter ID, NREGA job card, National Population Register letter, validated phone number, and bank or trading account details. For B2B entities, the RBI Master Direction on KYC 2016 Customer Due Diligence process must be followed.
sections.retrieval_text: Annexure III to the CERT-In Directions 2022 specifies the KYC requirements applicable to virtual asset service providers, virtual asset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers under Direction (vi). For individual customers, Officially Valid Documents include a passport, driving licence, proof of Aadhaar, Voter Identity Card issued by the Election Commission of India, NREGA job card signed by a State Government officer, National Population Register letter, validated phone number, and trading account or bank account details. KYC procedures must follow the RBI KYC Directions 2016, SEBI circular dated April 24 2020, or DoT S-KYC notice dated September 21 2021. For business entity customers, the Customer Due Diligence process under the RBI Master Direction on KYC 2016 must be followed and maintained.
